data.title: Carillon Scout Mid Cap Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview

The Russell Midcap® Index’s return was positive in the second quarter despite a sharp selloff in early April following President Trump’s “Liberation Day” tariff announcement. The weakness in equities, bonds and the U.S. dollar following the announcement likely convinced the president to delay collecting additional tariffs, which provided more time for trading partners to negotiate better terms. Investors sensed a standoff on tariffs, so the focus shifted to the “One Big Beautiful Bill” and deregulation, both of which investors view more favorably. Sector returns for the index were mostly higher, though the more defensive consumer staples and real estate sectors were down. The energy sector reported the worst results for the index as oil producers and service companies performed poorly despite Middle East geopolitical events in June that temporarily pushed up oil prices.

The macroeconomic environment was relatively steady, with gross domestic product (GDP) estimates bouncing back from a weak reading in the first quarter. The U.S. Federal Reserve (Fed) remained on hold as it monitored the impact on inflation from tariffs. Unemployment remained low, though weekly unemployment claims trended higher. The employment picture looked much like the overall economy, in a standoff, as employers wait and see on fiscal and monetary policy.

Investment Team

Derek Smashey, CFA, Lead Portfolio Manager

John Indellicate, CFA, Portfolio Co-Manager

Jason Votruba, CFA, Portfolio Co-Manager

Eric Chenoweth, CFA, Portfolio Co-Manager

Characteristics

Total Net Assets (billions): $3.09

Number of holdings: 122

Portfolio Review

Top securities

ATI produces high-performance materials for the aerospace and defense markets as well as for critical applications in electronics, medical, and specialty energy. ATI has transformed itself into a producer of high-quality nickel and titanium alloys and shed its exposure to commoditized stainless steel sheet metals, achieving an aerospace mix that generates a majority of overall revenues. Also boosting investor interest have been a rising aerospace production outlook, new business awards from a large European jet manufacturer, and improved execution of more value-added engine and airframe parts. We believe ATI should deserve to trade more in line with other aerospace parts suppliers as the company proves it can produce more consistent revenue growth at higher margins. Potential opportunities are also developing around fusion and nuclear energy.

Cloudflare (NET) provides internet infrastructure services that enhance website and application performance, security, and reliability. The stock performed well as the company showed solid progress in enterprise penetration as well as cloud security adoption. For example, Cloudflare won a record-sized deal for its Workers platform over a larger competitor, along with several multiyear large cloud security contracts during the quarter. Its financial metrics have also improved, helped by shortening sales cycles, stabilizing churn rate, and improving net-new annual recurring revenue growth. In our view, Cloudflare is one of the most competitive artificial intelligence (AI) inferencing platforms in the market, and we expect the business to grow rapidly for the next few years along with steady margin expansion.

Quanta Services provides infrastructure solutions to leading energy developers and large multistate utilities. Quanta is winning new business tied to the build out of AI power infrastructure as well as marquee renewable energy projects. The onshoring of manufacturing capacity and growth in electric vehicles are also driving increased electricity demand. There are several large transmission projects that Quanta is constructing, which we expect should provide revenue growth for years to come.

Celestica (CLS) is a contract manufacturer focused primarily on providing data center hardware components as well as aerospace and defense and industrial parts. The company reported impressive quarterly results in a highly uncertain macroeconomic environment driven primarily by strong demand for networking switches, with demand for 400-gigabyte switches being persistently strong while a ramp-up of demand for 800-gigabyte switches provided the upside. White box solutions in the networking industry are gaining share against branded vendors due largely to their lower total cost of ownership and more flexible designs. We forecast that estimates will move up throughout the year as we believe the company should continue to win new deals. In addition, we expect that the existing hyperscaler programs should ramp quicker than anticipated, which may provide further earnings upside.

GE Vernova (GEV) provides technologies and services for generating, converting, storing, and managing electricity, including gas, nuclear, wind, solar, and grid solutions. As with Quanta Services, investors see significant electricity demand growth led by AI data centers, in addition to other drivers. Led by increasing demand for gas turbines, earnings expectations continue to trend higher as GE Vernova’s backlog already stretches into the next decade. Also, as nuclear power is being reconsidered, nuclear power plant turbines and small modular reactors could add additional growth. We see significant opportunities for this market leader to support power demand growth.

Bottom securities

Viper Energy owns mineral and royalty interests in oil- and gas-producing acreage in the Permian Basin of West Texas and southeastern New Mexico. Oil prices, which drive the company’s sales, were weak during the quarter. Fears of near-term oversupply weighed on the oil market, as the Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries (OPEC) increased output. Despite the near-term concerns for oil, we think the market will regain some health when OPEC finishes its output hikes later this year. We believe Viper remains a capital-efficient and cash-generative way to invest in oil and gas.

Zimmer Biomet Holdings (ZBH), which manufactures and sells medical implants for joint replacements, lagged. Despite an overall solid earnings report, Zimmer lowered guidance due to dilution from a recent acquisition and the estimated impact from announced but not yet implemented tariffs. The company also lost some market share in its knee replacement product. We view the lowered guidance as a one-time reset that should likely reverse given the recent news around tariffs, and we think that an upcoming new release cycle should help accelerate Zimmer’s revenue growth.

Mid-America Apartment Communities (MAA) owns and operates multifamily housing in the Sunbelt. Rent growth has been stagnant in the region, as new supply deliveries have depressed pricing. There have also been concerns about population declines in the Sunbelt from more deportations and potential legal action around how competing apartments set pricing. We think those concerns are mostly overblown and expect rent pricing to improve as the industry has already moved past the peak of new supply coming to market.

Coca-Cola (KO) Consolidated is one of the largest Coca-Cola bottlers in the United States. First-quarter earnings were a bit disappointing as higher costs related to bottled water and aluminum weighed on profit margins. Given how the company’s brands tracked at store scanners over the quarter, we expect earnings to improve for the balance of the year. Coca-Cola Consolidated is rare in that it not only distributes Coke products, but also Dr. Pepper and Dunkin Donuts products, giving it denser and less competitive routes than most peers.

Aftermarket auto parts supplier LKQ (LKQ) delivered lackluster performance. As the cost of repairing cars has increased, the number of cars that are being repaired, which is the source of demand for LKQ’s domestic operations, has declined. Bad weather in Europe and unclear messaging around the impact of tariffs also weighed on the stock following its first-quarter report. We believe the tariff fears will subside, and with management’s increased focus on execution and margin, earnings results should be more attractive going forward.

Outlook

We think risk-adjusted opportunities are balanced for the broad mid-cap indices after the recent market rally. It’s fascinating how quickly market sentiment can change. In just a few months, investor sentiment went from extreme pessimism to optimism. Crowding into more defensive, lower-volatility stocks in early April gave way to bidding up speculative stocks tied to cryptocurrencies, nonprofitable technology, and retail investor favorites in June. The quarter led off with a significant tariff scare, so the defensive positioning initially looked prescient. Trump quickly pivoted, however, postponing many of his tariffs for 90 days and essentially stopping government efficiency efforts led by the U.S. Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE). We were somewhat defensively positioned early in the quarter but quickly repositioned the portfolio more aggressively when Trump announced the tariff delay. At the time, numerous stocks were oversold due to macroeconomic concerns, which presented an interesting opportunity. Now, with the Russell Midcap Index up more than 20% from the lows, much of the risk has been priced out of the market, in our view.

We see the market as a bit complacent as exhibited by the Chicago Board Options Exchange (CBOE) Volatility Index (VIX), which is back to February levels, and the low put-to-call ratio. Both metrics provide insight into how options investors see future volatility and risk. Some investor sentiment indicators are also pointing to optimism, such as the CNN Fear and Greed Index. We understand why the market has a sanguine outlook – inflation is coming down, which may give the Fed a chance to cut interest rates later this year; the One Big Beautiful Bill should drive fiscal expansion; and both the U.S. dollar and oil prices are moving lower. We can debate the long-term fiscal implications of these developments, but in the near term they tend to support domestic equity markets.

In many cases, stocks with good fundamentals (and in some cases just a good story) are rather fully valued. Of course, these stocks may continue to move higher if there’s no reason for capital to rotate into out-of-favor companies. Stocks that appear favorably priced based on our work tend to suffer from various headwinds, such as companies tied to weak housing fundamentals, non-digital advertising, offline retail, and renewable energy and oilfield services, among others. In these circumstances, we will remain patient as we dig for stocks with under-appreciated earnings power and long-term competitive advantages at reasonable prices.

The Carillon Scout Mid Cap Fund is avoiding significant macroeconomic calls at the start of the third quarter. We acknowledge the policy uncertainty from tariffs and geopolitical events (e.g., Iran, Ukraine), but we balance this against the potential for favorable fiscal and monetary support, which often outweighs other factors. As mentioned earlier, valuations look full for companies with good fundamentals, and fundamentals look weak for those with attractive valuations.

With this starting point, we are for now focusing less on the top-down outlook in favor of our bottom-up analysis and are sticking with themes we view as attractive. We maintained exposure to AI infrastructure holdings as the AI capital expenditure outlook is better than we expected a few months back. We have some exposure to companies offering AI applications, but phase 2 of the AI supercycle is developing slower than most expected, as AI-infused software applications, fully autonomous agents, and other products beyond chatbots remain in beta testing. Humanoid robotics is an interesting theme, and we expect some of our semiconductor and industrial companies to see significant growth from this innovation in coming years. It’s yet another demand boost for semiconductors and electricity. We see little priced in from robotics, so as this becomes a meaningful revenue source, these stocks may react positively.

We continue to see opportunities in domestic infrastructure, especially energy and electricity. Tariffs only accelerate reshoring and could lead to even more infrastructure spending. Consumer companies that are more isolated from tariff impacts, such as those that provide experiences or that provide good consumer value, also remain attractive. We see potential opportunities in retail, restaurants, and entertainment when there’s a strong consumer value proposition and significant long-term growth potential. As always, we prefer to own bottom-up, long-term compounders at attractive prices, but we must be aware of the political, economic, and monetary backdrop to protect capital and identify opportunities.

Definitions:

Aftermarket refers to parts or accessories that are not produced by an original equipment manufacturer but are designed and sold to be used with or in place of the manufacturer’s parts.

Artificial intelligence (AI) is technology that enables computers and machines to simulate human learning, comprehension, problem solving, decision making, creativity and autonomy. AI inferencing refers to an AI model producing its own predictions or conclusions or working on its own to solve a task.

Beta testing refers to the field testing of a new product with real users.

Call options are financial contracts that give the buyer of the option the right, but not the obligation, to buy a stock, bond, commodity, or other instrument – known as the underlying asset – at a specified price (the “strike price”) within a specific time period (the expiration or time to maturity). The buyer profits when the underlying asset increases in price.

Capital expenditures, or capex, are monies used by a company to buy, improve, or maintain physical assets such as real estate, facilities, technology, or equipment, and may include new projects or investments.

Churn refers to customers who stop doing business with a company. The churn rate is commonly expressed as a percentage, for example, of service subscribers who cancel their subscriptions during a given period.

The CNN Fear & Greed Index gauges stock market movements to try to determine whether stocks are fairly priced. It assumes that excessive fear tends to drive down share prices, and too much greed tends to have the opposite effect. It is calculated using seven indicators that measure some aspect of stock market behavior. They are market momentum, stock price strength, stock price breadth, put and call options, junk bond demand, market volatility, and safe haven demand. The index tracks how much these individual indicators deviate from their averages compared to how much they normally diverge. The index gives each indicator equal weighting in calculating a score from 0 to 100, with 100 representing maximum greediness and 0 signaling maximum fear.

Contract manufacturers produce products or parts of products for other companies to use and sell in their businesses.

Defensive positioning often is characterized by rebalancing the investment portfolio regularly to maintain an intended asset allocation. They also typically entail investing in high-quality, short-maturity bonds and blue-chip stocks, diversifying across sectors and countries, and holding cash and cash equivalents in down markets.

Defensive sectors include companies that tend to have a constant demand for their products or services, making their operations more stable during different phases of the business cycle.

Defensive stocks provide consistent dividends and stable earnings regardless of whether the overall stock market is rising or falling. Companies with shares considered to be defensive tend to have a constant demand for their products or services and thus their operations are more stable during different phases of the business cycle.

Dilution or dilutive refers to a decrease in the value of an area of an investment portfolio due to the adverse effects of influences such as portfolio allocation and security selection.

The U.S. Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE) was established by an executive order that renamed the U.S. Digital Service (USDS). Its stated goal is to maximize governmental efficiency and productivity.

Enterprise penetration refers to the process of testing an organization’s computer vulnerabilities by cyber-security professionals.

Fiscal policy refers to the tax collection and spending a government uses to influence its country’s economy.

Gross domestic product (GDP) is the total value of goods and services provided in an economy during a specified period, often one quarter or one year.

Guidance refers to statements from the managers of publicly traded companies that indicate whether they expect to realize near-term profits or losses and why.

Headwind is a term used to describe events or market forces that hinder the prospects for performance in an investment or investments.

Humanoid robotics refers to the construction of robots with human-like parts and features, such as arms, legs, torsos, and heads, as well as the ability to mimic certain human movements.

Hyperscalers refers to the largest cloud computing providers that can provide massive amounts of computing resources and storage at enterprise scale.

Liberation Day is a term used by President Donald Trump to refer to April 2, 2025, when he announced a wide range of unexpectedly high tariffs on many U.S. trading partners, triggering a global selloff of risk assets.

Macroeconomic refers to the branch of economics that focuses on seeking to understand the interactions between the markets, businesses, governments, and consumers that make up an entire economy.

Margin expansion refers to a rise in the percentage of revenue that a company earns as profit.

Monetary policy refers to the decisions made by central banks to raise or lower benchmark interest rates or otherwise tighten or loosen credit to influence an economy’s growth, inflation, or employment levels.

Net-new annual recurring revenue refers to the net impact of new revenue gained from acquiring new customers on the annual revenues that a company receives over the course of a year.

Network switches are pieces of computer hardware that connect devices and relay packets of data to specified destinations in a computer network. Switch metrics such as 400 gigabyte and 800 gigabyte refer to the speed at which the switches are capable of transferring data.

The One Big Beautiful Bill Act, passed and signed into law in July 2025, raised the U.S. debt ceiling by $5 trillion, made permanent tax cuts created by the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act of 2017, and changed a wide range of other aspects of federal tax policy, made changes to health insurance legislation, phased out or reduced credits for clean energy production or use, among other things.

Onshoring describes an effort to bring manufacturing and other services back to the United States from overseas operations.

An option is a financial instrument based on the value of underlying securities such as stocks. An options contract offers its buyers the opportunity to buy or sell — depending on the type of contract they hold — the underlying asset.

The Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries, also known as OPEC, was founded in 1960 and is a permanent organization of 13 oil-exporting developing nations that coordinates the petroleum policies of its member countries, which are Iran, Iraq, Kuwait, Saudi Arabia, Venezuela, Libya, the United Arab Emirates, Algeria, Nigeria, Gabon, Angola, Equatorial Guinea, and Congo.

Oversold is a term used to describe a security or group of securities believed to be trading at a level below its or their intrinsic or fair value.

Overweight describes a portfolio position in an industry sector or some other category that is greater than the corresponding weight level in a benchmark portfolio.

Put options are financial contracts that give the buyer of the option the right, but not the obligation, to sell a specified amount of a stock, bond, commodity, or other instrument at a pre-determined price (the “strike price’) within a specified time frame. Put options increase in value as the price of the underlying asset falls, as the volatility in the price of the underlying asset increases, or as interest rates decline.

The put-to-call ratio is a measure of market sentiment that reflects whether investors expect prices to rise or fall. It is calculated by comparing the volumes of puts and calls purchased. Bearish markets feature larger numbers of puts than calls. Bullish markets feature more calls than puts.

A release cycle refers to the process of planning, developing, testing, and deploying new products, updates or features.

Reshoring describes an effort to bring manufacturing and other services back to the United States from overseas operations.

Rotation refers to the movement of investments from one industry, sector, factor, or asset class to another as market participants react to or try to anticipate the next stage of the economic cycle.

A supercycle is a sustained period of strong economic growth that drives a prolonged level of demand for the goods or services beyond what companies can deliver.

Underweight describes a portfolio position in an industry sector or some other category that is less than the corresponding weight level in a benchmark portfolio.

The VIX – officially known as the Chicago Board Options Exchange (CBOE) Volatility Index – is a real-time market index that represents the market’s expectation of 30-day forward-looking volatility. Derived from the price inputs of the S&P 500 ((SP500), (SPX)) index options, it provides a measure of market risk and investors’ sentiments.

White box solutions refer to generic, customizable and often less expensive computer hardware or software products in place of more expensive proprietary brand name products.

Indices

The Russell Midcap® Index, the Fund’s benchmark, measures the performance of the mid-cap segment of the U.S. equity universe. The stocks are also members of the Russell 1000 Index.

Investors cannot invest directly in an index and unmanaged index returns do not reflect any fees, expenses or sales charges.

data.title: Devon Energy: The Best Opportunity In US Oil Today That I'm Buying
data.description:
Introduction & Financials

Devon Energy Corporation (NYSE:DVN) is a rapidly growing US oil and gas company focusing on onshore operations predominantly in the Delaware Basin, Williston and Eagle Ford. After a few recent acquisitions (see 2024 and 2022, among others), as well as a big merger with WPX Energy completed in 2021 and significant investments, they delivered a record volume last year, with a massive investment cycle still underway, which is not a bad outcome to achieve from the upside of the cycle by themselves - but I also think there is high potential to see this being an acquisition target, as I will mention below.

I believe that if they manage to solidify or “protect” this level of production when prices fall (like today) and then grow it again in the next upcycle - especially in gas and other fuels - this can definitely do much better. As we can see, Q2 already delivered better-than-expected volumes, spending and also significant cash flows, Devon continuing to deliver these improvements at relatively the same stock price as a result of pressure from falling oil prices. These also led to an outlook improvement to 825,000 to 842,000 Boe per day in 2025.

What’s setting Devon apart is the massive amount of money spent on investments made in recent years and even ramped up in 2024, reaching ~$7.43 billion in CAPEX. It may not look like a lot compared to giants like Exxon Mobil (XOM) that was at $19.32 billion last year, but Exxon is a ~$463 billion market cap company compared to Devon’s $21.5 billion.

They had a negative cash flow last year due to massive CAPEX, but it’s worth noting that they aren’t really losing money on an operational level, being able to reduce this CAPEX when required, expecting to spend around $3.7 billion (at midpoint; $3.6 billion to $3.8 billion expected) as a result of the falling oil prices, a $400 million adjustment from the 2024 guidance, showing that they are proactive and rather careful, not wanting to go into significant debt or dilute us significantly to fund these projects. And through all of that capital cut, they still expect an even better production in 2025, which shows significant capital efficiency improvements.

As a result of these investments and improvements, they’re also driving their costs down and improving the capital efficiencies significantly, with a 12% improvement in the Delaware operations in H1 2025 compared to FY 2024. Despite being American, they also have a relatively low breakeven cost, which is an advantage especially in a bad market. At around $70 per barrel, they were aiming at producing about $3 billion in cash flow with around $4 billion in CAPEX, which is amazing for a $21.5 billion market cap from an American company - again, I want to emphasize that American assets tend to be much more expensive compared to pretty much anything else, and P/FCF a ratio of ~7 (without even accounting the growth potential from the massive amounts of investments) is something you see in much riskier regions. Still, Q2 actually came with a very solid free cash flow of $589 million, and that came through significant price volatility due to Trump’s tariffs, and it might fall as a result of the lowest oil prices we’ve seen in recent history now.

As of the latest quarter, they provided another very useful free cash flow estimate. With the $3.7 billion CAPEX they expect now, they see a FCF ranging from $2.5 billion with WTI at $55, $3 billion at $65 and $3.7 billion at $75, all of which would be very good for a US-based producer that’s still investing massive amounts of money - plus the potential beyond $75.

Not only that, but the company still sees very significant business optimization opportunities in many areas. They estimate even $1 billion in run-rate improvements by the end of 2026 through improvements in capital efficiency, production, commercial opportunities and corporate cost reductions, which I’d see as an extra green flag on top of the already solid cash flows even in a downcycle.

Financially, based on their latest report, we see a great position, with the current assets covering the current liabilities and significant improvements in equity YoY. We see $15.29 billion in equity on a $21.5 billion market cap, which is a PB of ~1.4, which is not bad especially for American producers, which tend to have more expensive assets. It’s also worth mentioning that their NAV is likely different today, but that fluctuates based on oil prices, at the end of the day. If we see low prices for longer, then there’s also likely to see companies go into a self-preservation mode rather than look for consolidation (mergers and acquisitions).

The debt is spread very well into the future, and they also have a $2.5 billion debt reduction program underway. It’s important to note that ~70% of their outstanding debt obligations mature after 2030, so they have plenty of time to efficiently refinance and pay down these liabilities, with no rush in the immediate future, especially if we get rate cuts that lower their interest payments.

Dividends & Buybacks

As usual, we see them paying significant dividends during the good times and now cutting them to a yield of ~2.84, but what sets Devon apart is the fact that they actually ramped up the buybacks now ($5 billion buyback program currently active), when the stock fell.

They target returning up to 70% in dividends and buybacks but focusing on buybacks for now, which I think is great. That should be around $2.1 billion in total with oil at $65, so a roughly 10% total yield on the current price, which is definitely impressive. I have to appreciate that they ramped up the buybacks as the stock fell and focused on developing instead of doing buybacks at all-time highs when they had the money, like many other commodity producers generally do (and others, not only them), so that’s definitely another plus, for me.

Risks & Opportunities

Now, following the great oil upcycle seen in recent years, pretty much everyone is focusing on improving the margins and the balance sheet while growth usually comes from a bit of exploration and especially the consolidation mentioned earlier. As a note, virtually every oil giant has been undergoing at least one significant merger/acquisition over the past years: Exxon's (XOM) Pioneer deal, Chevron (CVX) buying Hess, Occidental (OXY) + CrownRock, ConocoPhillips + Marathon Oil (COP), and even Devon’s 3 relatively large M&As seen since 2021 mentioned earlier. As mentioned in the beginning, I believe that given the efficiency, growth and overall great and relatively cheap business that Devon is today, it wouldn’t be impossible for a company to pursue buying them. It could help many players expand their exposure to cheap US production at an attractive price, although I think it would be worse for Devon, the company having significant potential even by itself.

This however adds nothing to the total supply, as the production barely changes (unless the merged company/buyer does some cuts or improvements), which can have a positive effect on the price of oil while these companies still manage to develop on paper (with per share values changing or not depending on how good of a deal they got). This makes sense to some degree since a project is usually thought of over the long-term, and at this point, oil might still face uncertainty regarding its long-term demand.

Even if the companies might not present it that way, oil demand is expected to peak around early to mid 2030s, making the whole exploration, feasibility and production process kind of risky. From funding and finding an oil project, it can take even a decade until you begin to make your money back. That’s why even Exxon is now aiming to invest up to $30 billion in green and energy transition commodities from 2025 to 2030, despite Trump taking office. Plus, as I mention below, new oil production will be more expensive, so you are already taking a higher risk by investing now into an unknown future, unlike something like iron or copper, which will most likely be here for the foreseeable future thanks to the vital roles they play and limited commercially-viable replaceability.

However, this is where natural gas comes into play, which could more than offset the potential fall in oil volumes as it can be an ideal transition fuel. As a note, Devon even entered into a 10-year agreement with Centrica (OTCPK:CPYYF) (OTCPK:CPYYY) to supply gas to them a few days ago. Since they’re producing it in the US, this could come with significant potential, especially after Trump’s $750 billion LNG deal with the EU that is, as I expected, very beneficial for American LNG in the long-term, with Trump seemingly wanting to solidify the US as Russia’s replacement when it comes to supplying gas to the EU.

Still, in the short-term, oil and gas will remain very volatile, and so will Devon and every other commodity producer. An opportunity in the near-term should also come from rate cuts, which should increase the demand for oil and gas as they would boost economic activity. Especially with a potential resolution to Trump’s tariffs and China’s policy shift that should also boost their economy, there is potential to see a significant boost in oil demand, as the US remains a key player both in the supply and the demand of this commodity.

Also, as I mentioned, China’s policy change announced last year represents the first easing since 2010, with strong fiscal stimulus, rate cuts, $140 billion injected into the financial system, asset purchases and more, which could be great for the country’s economy and its demand for oil and gas. This can be great especially for their demand coming from petrochemicals, which could be key for its “next stage” of oil demand growth. Petrochemicals (such as propylene and ethylene) are used for various plastics and synthetic fibres, which we know very well that the country then sells everywhere on the planet. And, even better, global rate cuts could be very beneficial for China directly, as that increases disposable budgets and ultimately boosts the demand for Chinese products worldwide. I recently mentioned this quote that I think gives us a better idea about the magnitude of these, written by Ciarán Healy for the IEA:

The speed and scale of the expansion of China’s petrochemical sector dwarfs any historical precedent, roughly doubling the pace of earlier capacity additions in the Middle East and United States. Between 2019 and 2024, China is set to add as much production capacity for ethylene and propylene – the two most important petrochemical building blocks – as presently exists in Europe, Japan and Korea combined.

This is a very significant change that’s been happening even during the pandemic, basically preparing China for a post-COVID and post-rate cuts environment that could help boost the demand for their products on a global scale, while other countries still face trouble (see Germany, who just reported another quarter of economic downfall, being down 0.3% in Q2 - although we should note that the new Chancellor’s administration made revitalizing it a priority).

In March 2025, Devon’s CEO was succeeded, having served in the role since the WPX Energy merger of equals back in January 2021 (plus 7 years of leading that company). This can be an opportunity, but also comes with uncertainty because you never know what new $1 billion skyscraper this CEO will want (a reference to the one built over a decade ago in Oklahoma that cost ~$750 million, or about $1 billion today - which even has its own Wikipedia page).

Of course, as with any oil company, it’s important to keep in mind that their key commodity depends a lot on global politics/economics/etc. OPEC+ has been ramping up production for the past few months, Trump’s tariffs could slow down the economy, Israel-Iran conflict could continue to escalate, Fed could cut the rates soon - all of these have significant effects on oil especially in the short-term. Understanding and being ready for this kind of volatility is key if you want to invest in oil stocks, because I believe these potential swings offer great long-term opportunities, as I’ll mention in the valuation segment. Buying a stock like Devon at something like $20 during bad times and significant fear could result in very significant long-term returns from a healthy company that’s expanding significantly.

And another important thing mentioned before that I want to remind you is that cheap oil is very limited now - and running out. Especially in the US, outside of the Permian (where the Delaware Basin is; current known reserves estimated to run for another 15 to 20 more years at current production, although discoveries can change that) and a few other spots, break-evens are expected to get even to $50 to $70 or even more in the future. And the US in particular has a huge impact on the price thanks both to the supply and demand. This could have significant effects on oil prices throughout the cycle. Ten years from now, we could be looking at “bottoms” of $50/bbl instead of the $30/bbl we’ve seen during 2016, and the production cost of most companies would adjust.

I believe the ultimate winners here should be the companies/countries with enough cheap reserves (Exxon, Shell and others are developing new projects with ~$35/bbl break-evens in countries such as Guyana), such as Saudi Arabia. This could be a reason to focus on green alternatives, which are getting even 90% cheaper over the past decade, as even Exxon is doing. Still, less demand for oil in the long-term doesn’t have to mean lower prices, although it’s likely that the more expensive production spots will be the first to go due to the weaker margins until the market reaches a supply and demand balance.

Still, once again, the company depends on oil prices at the end of the day, and the supply and demand matters significantly. We see the EIA forecasting stagnation in US oil production in the near future, with the Permian remaining the key source, of course. Still, a stagnation is not a decrease, which could signal a really bad environment for oil companies (see 2015-2018 commodity downside), as it would come as a result of low prices that are below breakeven costs.

But, we might be getting into the bad times for oil-related industries, as bad times lead to CAPEX-cutting quite fast. For example, a recent Dallas Fed Energy Survey showed that to profitably drill a new well in the Permian Basin, companies needed prices of ~$64 per barrel, which is very close to the level we are at today:

For the entire sample, firms need $64 per barrel on average to profitably drill, higher than the $62-per-barrel price when this question was asked last year. Across regions, average breakeven prices to profitably drill range from $59 to $70 per barrel. Breakeven prices in the Permian Basin average $65 per barrel, $4 higher than last year. Almost all firms in the survey can profitably drill a new well at current prices. (The WTI spot price was $83 per barrel during the survey period.)

Peer Comparison

Compared to peers such as Occidental Petroleum (OXY), ConocoPhillips (COP), Diamondback Energy (FANG), Exxon Mobil (XOM), Chevron (CVX) and Coterra Energy (CTRA), we actually see that Devon is one of the cheapest from almost every point of view, especially the Price/Cash Flow which is very low - even ~3 times less than Chevron or Exxon. The company seems to be trading at even cheaper ratios than Warren Buffett’s OXY, which I analyzed recently, and given Devon’s operational excellence and proactive strategy displayed over the years, I don’t see a reason why this should remain. Given the valuation of the larger peers, which are helped especially on index demand thanks to their size, I think seeing them at least try to buy DVN shouldn't be impossible, as mentioned earlier.

Valuation

Assuming oil stabilizing at around $65, we can use a starting FCF of $3 billion in 2025 based on the current CAPEX forecast, and adding $300 million in 2026 coming from lower CAPEX and a minimal effect from their $1 billion cost savings (that’s $1 billion pre-tax, but I just want to be even safer to prove a point), and growing at a 4% CAGR for the first 5 years to account for rate cuts boosting demand and lowering interest payments, then a 3% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years, a 2% terminal growth rate to account for potential issues in oil demand and a WACC of 8%, above my 7.08% estimate (assumes: 8.01%, 5.62% cost of debt, 21% corporate tax rate) for a better margin of safety, although rate cuts will result in a lower number, of course. The resulting DCF valuation for DVN yields an enterprise value of approximately $40.35 billion, and after subtracting the $7.12 billion in net debt, this returns an equity value of roughly $33.23 billion. With 636 million shares outstanding, this gives an intrinsic value of about $52.24 per share, which is more than 50% above the current price, not accounting for dividends and the potential effect of buybacks.

But, let’s be even more conservative and assume oil at $55 per barrel (when many players would have issues and be forced to cut CAPEX and lower their activity), when the company expects to make around $2.5 billion with current CAPEX - not accounting for any cost savings, CAPEX cuts or reduced interest. That’s a price of $41.83, which is still nearly 25% above the current price, and we also have significant buybacks and dividends on top.

Conclusions

Overall, Devon seems like a Strong Buy now, being one of the best opportunities in US oil and gas right now, especially as a smaller option that has high potential to improve, great financials and low-cost production, all coming at a low valuation compared to peers. As the CAPEX normalizes and they get back to a positive cash flow, they can continue with the significant buybacks and nice dividend on top. Having a ~10% total yield from an American company is definitely attractive, especially since they can do that sustainably even in this market thanks to the low valuation. Of course, this seems like a great position to add especially during downturns, and I intend to keep adding if the short-term noise continues.

data.title: Prime Energy Resources: The Place To Be During Uncertain Times
data.description:
Prime Energy Resources (NASDAQ:PNRG) has one of the best balance sheets in the business. The long-term debt is listed at roughly $12 million in the latest quarter. That is a level that even the latest reduced cash flow can easily handle in less than a quarter. This is the kind of company that makes it through whatever uncertainty or possible recession that may lie ahead (which Mr. Market seems to be increasingly worried about). I also believe that the "name" partners help to reduce the risk of an investment in a company like this one because those kinds of partners can evaluate a company far more thoroughly than most of us can. However, it came about, and it would appear to be a plus for this rather small producer.

The latest report also mentioned that a fully depreciated rig was sold for a six-figure gain. It definitely takes more than one transaction to be sure. But a gain like that may well point in the direction of conservative accounting compared to a lot of public companies.

Furthermore, this company does not do "road shows" as a general rule to promote the company. While that may frustrate some investors, it likely also points towards "letting the results do the talking." That is unlikely to be bad in the long run.

Major Shareholder Agreement

One unusual event is that the company announced that major shareholders got together to allow the CEO of the company to control roughly 80% of the outstanding shares. As to whether that is good or bad remains to be seen. But even before that announcement, it would appear that Mr. Charles E. Drimal, Jr., Chairman, CEO, and President, has firm control of the company through his personal holdings, as shown below:

To me, this would mean that nothing has materially changed in terms of company control, even with the major shareholder announcement.

As an aside, one major shareholder, Robert de Rothschild, has been steadily selling shares in the current fiscal year. On the NASDAQ website, his current holdings are evidently close to 230,000 shares (roughly).

Investor Implications

With roughly 80% of the shares outstanding off the market. That leaves about 20% of the shares for the public to trade. As such, investors need patience and limit orders. Liquidity for someone that wants to accumulate a "decent" position in the stock could mean that it will take some time to avoid overpaying.

Production

Production declined with the latest three- and six-month comparison. However, given the relatively low debt balance, this decline is really not much of an issue.

The cash flow would appear to be more than adequate to restore any production declines. There is also an adequate bank line in case some borrowing is needed.

This company has a fair number of partners; even though it does manage some of the production itself, it would be speculative as to whether this company controls production levels of a significant amount of its production.

Furthermore, it may be wise to follow the "lead" of some of its partners for bringing online new production as commodity prices have weakened considerably in the production comparison shown above.

Cash Flow

Cash flow has held up rather well for this small producer.

As shown above, there was a fair amount of debt repaid in the latest quarter.

But the most noticeable thing was that there was still a fair amount of money spent on capital projects. Admittedly, the current amount is down from the previous fiscal year. But the level of spending is really significant for a company of this size.

More importantly, for the production shown previously, the level of cash flow is on the generous side. It tends to confirm the ability to run a debt-free balance sheet while spending what the company needs to be spent.

Most would blame the stock repurchases for exceeding the cash flow. However, the level of debt noted early in the article is really not all that much compared to the cash flow. The company may not technically be debt-free. But it is "close enough".

Summary

Prime Energy Resources did "shift gears" enough to deliver a strong natural gas production increase for the second quarter when compared to the previous fiscal year. But results were still dominated by the oil commodity prices and production decline. However, that production shift does show some flexibility that has the ability to influence profitability in the future.

This company is located close enough to the growing LNG export ability that it may be one of many Texas companies to benefit from that growing export ability.

The stock remains a strong buy. Sometimes small companies are run conservatively enough that they can be a safer place to invest than some of the larger competitors. That appears to be the case here.

Finances are rock solid, with almost no long-term debt. The company is in an excellent position to "go shopping" for decent distressed assets if management wants to do that. Furthermore, without any "road shows," this stock is unlikely to ever be overpriced. Instead, it may well remain reasonably priced throughout the company's existence. The share buyback program limits downside risk as well.

There is one insider that has been selling material amounts of shares. But large shareholders banded together to give the CEO voting rights for about 80% of the stock. Sometimes stock sales have an offsetting event, as appears to be the case here.

Once the current period of pricing weakness is over, it will be interesting to see what management does with the assets and the various partnerships. The interests in some partnerships with "name" partners give this small producer a credibility it might not otherwise have.

Risks

With any small company, the key risk is likely to be the loss of service of key personnel. Generally, the people running smaller companies are a sort of "jack of all trades" that are extremely hard to replace if their services are lost. Here, such an event could materially impair the company's ability to function or result in a sale of the company.

On the other hand, the low debt strategy lowers financial risks tremendously. When that strategy is combined with the ability to partner with some of the bigger names in the industry, then a lot of small company risks are greatly reduced. In fact, the business strategy chosen here probably reduces investment risk compared to many larger companies. A material change in either the financial strategy or the business strategy would likely result in a complete re-evaluation of the investment merits.

data.title: Hewlett Packard Enterprise: Networking To Take Off After The Juniper Integration
data.description:
I initiate my coverage on Hewlett Packard Enterprise Company (NYSE:HPE) with a strong buy rating. My bull case stands on two main pillars: after the Juniper acquisition, I expect the networking segment to anchor profits, and AI orders, backlog, and revenue have been ticking up, and most importantly, I expect the momentum in AI to continue. Management expects the combined networking unit to contribute more than half of operating income and be EPS accretive in year one.

On the AI product side, Q2 revenue reached $1.0B, up over 10% sequentially, with $1.1B in new orders and $3.2B backlog. Intelligent Edge revenue also turned up to $1.2B in Q2, up 8% year over year and 2% sequentially.

On the risk side, the server segment is weighing on margins. Margins in this segment were just 5.9% in Q2, with only mid to high single digits guided for Q3. Looking at valuation, in my view, the attractive valuation compared to sector medians is enough to overlook the fact that the company is trading above its five year average multiples.

Going into Q3 earnings, I believe the results will determine whether the share price breaks the resistance shown below. I see an ascending triangle pattern, which is often a sign of an inflection point going into earnings.

Overall, I believe the integration of Juniper will be accretive to the top and bottom line. Therefore, I expect the market to welcome the integration of Juniper into the consolidated results.

The Juniper Networks Acquisition

Let's first look at what the deal was in the first place. This was an all-cash acquisition for $40 per share (roughly $14 billion in equity value). This deal closed on July 2, 2025 after being first announced in January 2024. Since then, Juniper's former CEO, Rami Rahim, is now leading their combined "HPE Networking" business. This wasn't an easy acquisition, though. To settle the DOJ lawsuit and allow the closing, HPE agreed to two main actions.

First, they agreed to divest HPE's Instant On WLAN business to a DOJ-approved buyer within 180 days. And second, they agreed to auction a non exclusive license for Juniper's Mist AIOps source code to at least one rival (and two, if the bids are over $8 million). Even though I am not particularly excited about these two agreements, the upside that Juniper brings to HPE significantly outshadows the impact of these agreements.

The acquisition primarily expands HPE's networking franchise, historically reported in Intelligent Edge. According to the July press release, this is what the company expects from a financial perspective:

The acquisition of Juniper's high-margin business is expected to be accretive in the near- and long-term for the combined company. The transaction will be accretive to non-GAAP EPS in year 1, post close, with the combined networking business contributing more than 50% of total company operating income.

Looking at Juniper's latest 10-K, the company existed 2024 with $5.074B in revenue, $291.8M in operating income, at 5.8% operating margin. Furthermore, the company totaled $788 million in operating cash flow.

On HPE's side, the 2024 results for Intelligent Edge show $4.532B in revenue, $1.115B in operating income, at a 24.6% operating margin.

So, we are looking at a ~$9.6 billion networking revenue run rate base (before eliminations and before synergies/purchase-accounting effects). On the operating income, we are talking about a run rate of $1.4B. Given that HPE's consolidated operating income in FY 2024 was $2.19B, this is aligned with HPE's statement that the networking business will dominate the company's operating profit post close.

Overall, I feel optimistic about the completion of this acquisition. I am not alone, as Wall Street analysts seem to be content with the addition of Juniper.

AI Systems Momentum

On the AI systems segment, momentum is building up. This is visible especially in orders, conversions, backlog, and a near-term step-up in revenue.

In the past quarter, the company booked $1.1 billion in net new AI orders, including about a third of that in the enterprise segment, and a strong representation in sovereign. As seen in the table above, the company recognized $1B in AI revenue in Q2. That was up by over 10% sequentially, while the initial guidance was for a modest decline. Not bad, in my view.

Looking at the backlog, the company has $3.2 billion in AI systems revenue remaining. In Q3, they expect strong double-digit growth in revenue in this segment due to a large deal as they near the completion of the Blackwell NVL72 deployment.

Looking at the Intelligent Edge segment, I was pleasantly surprised by the recovery in revenue. Revenue came in at $1.2 billion, up 8% yoy, the first yoy increase in the past five quarters. Also, revenue was up by 2% sequentially.

I will keep a close eye on the expected performance of this segment, considering the Juniper acquisition. Overall, I expect an attrition in both the top line and operating profits; however, I believe the improvement will mainly materialize next year. As noted below, the Street significantly increased the top-line expectations for 2026.

On the bottom line, I also see an improvement, backweighted for 2026. In fact, the consensus points to a 24% yoy increase in EPS in FY 2026, compared to a decline of 6.5% this year.

Risks

In my view, the category that doesn't look so great is the margins in the Server segment. I believe this is one of the main pressures on the bottom line this year.

In Q2, the operating margin in the server segment was 5.9%, a decline of 510 bps yoy and 220 bps sequentially. I am not that worried about the yoy decline, considering that at the beginning of this year, HPE moved certain managed services out of the Server into the Hybrid Cloud segment. However, the sequential drop is indeed concerning, and it is a risk to my bull case if the company fails to lift margins in this segment by year end. Management is guiding operating margins in the server segment in Q3 in the mid-to-high single digits due to a higher mix of AI systems. Considering this guidance, it seems that a recovery to around 10% in this segment may not come until the exit of Q4 FY25.

On the revenue side, server sales went down by 500 bps sequentially, mainly due to lower traditional compute volumes after HPE tightened pricing (i.e., pulled back on discounting that hurt Q1). I am not that concerned with this decline, as better pricing (at the expense of lower volumes) should help margins in the near to mid term.

I will be keeping a close eye on the server segment, as I admit I am anything but content with the pressures on the bottom line. In the last quarter, this segment accounted for 53% of the total revenue. Yet, it only accounted for 36% of operating income. That has to improve.

Valuation

As seen below, the company is trading at attractive valuation multiples.

Looking at my favourite valuation metric, P/CF, this multiple is 33% below the sector median. That said, when compared to its 5-year historical range, it has increased significantly, currently trading at a multiple not seen since 2018.

On a P/S basis, I see a similar pattern. The multiple is 72% below the sector median, but 19% above its 5-year average. I believe the stock is getting expensive, but I wouldn't consider HPE to be overvalued yet.

Looking Ahead

Going into the back half of the year, I'd keep a close eye on the topline and margin guidance.

The company narrowed its FY25 revenue growth to 7-9% y/y. The midpoint is a mere 8%. Q3 revenue was guided in the range of $8.2 - $8.5B. This doesn't take into account the Juniper acquisition. As seen below, the Street does take into account this acquisition.

On margins, the company expects to exit Q4 above 30%, although for the full year, non-GAAP gross margins are expected to be below 30%. Finally, keep an eye on the non-GAAP EPS guidance for FY25, which was narrowed to $1.78-$1.90.

As for the Juniper acquisition, I would keep an eye on the two main terms that were settled in the DOJ case. I don't expect the company to break any of these terms; however, it is worth keeping a close eye on any developments.

data.title: IBIT: We're At The Top (Double Downgrade)
data.description:
Co-Authored by Noah Cox and Brock Heilig

Investment Thesis

I am lowering my rating on iShares Bitcoin Trust ETF (IBIT) from a strong buy to a hold. A big reason for this double downgrade is because of the fact that Bitcoin’s price has already surged roughly 600% from its cycle low of ~$16k to about $114k, which validated much of my earlier bull case.

It’s clear to me that most tailwinds I previously identified - rapid money supply expansion, favorable U.S. policy, and growing institutional adoption - have materialized and now appear fully baked into Bitcoin’s valuation. In my last article, I projected a price target of $148k for Bitcoin, assuming another 50-100% upside. At $114k, I believe those catalysts have largely run their course (while not pushing the price of the world’s largest cryptocurrency to the level I had hoped for).

I’ve also noticed that global liquidity conditions, which once propelled Bitcoin, are beginning to shift (Bitcoin’s price is no longer tracking as closely with the increase in the M2 money supply).

On top of this, there appears to be growing leverage within the Bitcoin ecosystem, especially debt-driven corporate purchases, which I believe introduces fragility for Bitcoin. Bitcoin has been in a 4 year boom-bust cycle basically since its inception. My fear is that we are at the peak of this cycle.

Normally, the adage for Bitcoin would be to hold till the next bull run. My fear is that this might be the last bull run. Technological threats like quantum computing were not central to my prior thesis, but they now cast a really longer-term shadow over Bitcoin’s core security. When we take into account all of these factors, it means that at $114k per Bitcoin, investors face significant downside if conditions deteriorate.

With this, shares are double downgraded from a strong buy to a hold.

Why I’m Doing Follow-Up Coverage

The biggest reason for my follow-up coverage of IBIT is because of what I think are red flags that have risen since the last time I wrote on the Bitcoin ETF. The market has shifted from predominantly spot-driven accumulation to heavy debt-funded buying from treasury companies, which changes the risk profile. On-chain data also suggests that a price slip could trigger cascading liquidations, unlike the earlier, more organic demand phase.

Another red flag I’ve seen come about is that companies are betting the balance sheet. Public firms like GameStop, MicroStrategy, Bit Digital, and Block have increasingly financed Bitcoin purchases via debt. For example, GameStop’s $1.3 billion convertible bond issuance in March was a big sign of euphoria, prompting a 25% drop in the company’s stock once risks became clear.

My previous article was contingent on the thesis that there were assumed strategic, unlevered Treasury allocations, not this level of corporate speculation.

The shift from organic to leveraged demand means the easy money from holding Bitcoin (and by extension IBIT) has been made and that further gains would come on borrowed capital and potentially borrowed time.

Leveraged Risk

One of the best-case studies in Bitcoin’s risk is MicroStrategy. The company holds over 600,000 Bitcoin - which is nearly 3% of the total supply. This Bitcoin is financed by roughly tranches of convertible bonds and preferred shares that now pay cash dividends. I wrote about this with Strategy (MSTR) but this does not feel sustainable.

This financing has set MicroStrategy up with more than $8 billion in debt and interest obligations that hinge on Bitcoin’s price remaining elevated. While shareholders enjoyed ~167% stock gains as Bitcoin rose, a downturn could really hurt the company and trigger a forced sell-off, risking broader market contagion.

It’s true that none of the Bitcoin that Strategy in particular is secured to debt, but at the end of the day, if any of their obligations default (such as a convertible bond or preferred stock preferred dividends), that could become a cascading issue.

Marathon Digital (MARA) is a different story. Marathon and other miners took high-interest loans secured by Bitcoin or mining rigs to expand operations. Because of this, a meaningful Bitcoin decline could lead to distressed collateral sales.

In bull markets, leverage acts like rocket fuel. In bear markets, it becomes gasoline on a fire.

Quantum Hacking Problems

Quantum hacking is starting to become a huge threat to Bitcoin’s cryptography. Bitcoin relies on the ECDSA (elliptic curve digital signature) algorithm, which conventional computers cannot break (requiring ~2^128 operations). However, a powerful quantum computer running Shor’s algorithm could solve this in polynomial time (exponentially less time than a classical computer). This means they can derive private keys from public keys within minutes. Once a Bitcoin address’ public key is revealed, I believe a quantum owned by an attacker will compute the private key and steal funds. This would be devastating for the cryptocurrency.

My concern looks like it will be real. There have been recent warnings that quantum computing breakthroughs may arrive sooner rather than later. In May, Dr. Elena Orlova (researcher at Google) suggested error-corrected qubits capable of breaking ECDSA could appear some time between 2027 and 2030 - years earlier than prior forecasts. The implication is that the Bitcoin ecosystem may need to transition to quantum-resistant algorithms within the next 5-7 years, not decades. This would also show up just as Bitcoin starts its next bull run cycle. This could kill the next bull run. With no new bull run, there is really no long-term reason to own Bitcoin.

Even if we don’t talk about quantum brute force hacking the whole blockchain, core legacy Bitcoin wallets are often vulnerable. Estimates suggest that over 60% of Bitcoin resides in vulnerable addresses from legacy addresses. When quantum computers reach critical capability, attackers could (and will) empty wallets using pure mathematics, undermining trust in the network. These wallets will have to be upgraded. This is the core problem.

Defending against these quantum attacks requires a fundamental protocol upgrade. Really, this will be a logistical nightmare (coordinating updates across miners, exchanges, wallets, etc.) There are also technical trade-offs, a timing dilemma, or really (because let's face it) hard fork implications.

Think about Satoshi Nakamoto’s wallet. Even the mysterious founder’s wallet is exposed since it used the older “pay-to-pubkey” architecture that exposed the wallet's public key during transactions.

This could be one of the biggest forks in the history of Bitcoin. I really don’t think this will go well. Some proposals indicate that there is a huge chunk of the blockchain that will have to be essentially frozen so that quantum computers don’t hack these wallets and dump this Bitcoin on the open market.

Valuation

I believe Bitcoin's recent range of $112k to $124k in recent weeks represents euphoria, which is roughly 7-8x above the bear-market low of $16k. At $114k, Bitcoin’s market cap is about $2.4 trillion. It's one of the top 10 largest assets on the planet.

Historical cycles show post-peak drawdowns of 70-80%. Treasury companies could be the catalyst that kicks off that downside.

Bitcoin’s price is closely tied to the global M2 money supply. While M2 surged through 2024 and 2025, forecasts project a global liquidity peak by mid-2026. As of today, in mid-2025, I think the easiest liquidity-driven gains are behind us. Any pivot toward liquidity tightening will be bad news for Bitcoin.

I also think cycle timing will play a big factor here. Each halving is a known catalyst to drive Bitcoin's price upward. Historically, peaks occur about 12-18 months post-halving. As of the time of this writing, we are about 16 months past the halving. Without a fresh fundamental catalyst, the odds favor a plateau or a pullback.

As I mentioned earlier in this article, my former Bitcoin price projection was $148k. However, at this point, a run from ~$114k to $148k (~29.8% further upside) would require either a liquidity surge or a new narrative drawing sidelined capital. With this, I view ~$124k as a de facto ceiling for Bitcoin in this cycle, barring any major developments.

Since I am a hold, I don’t know what the fair value of Bitcoin is at this point. I don’t think the remaining upside potential is worth it.

Bull Thesis

A quick note on the Bitcoin community’s main counter point.

Bitcoin enthusiasts all the way up to Strategy’s own Michael Saylor state that the core financial system will be faced with these same quantum threats.

Their core logic is the following:

Legacy finance will face the same quantum threats we will….

…so why are we singled out?

The key difference between Bitcoin and legacy finance: legacy finance is regulated and centralized, which makes upgrades to cryptography related to quantum easier.

Already key cybersecurity agencies like NIST (National Institute of Standards and Technology) have identified Post Quantum Algorithms for widespread usage to upgrade legacy IT networks in banking, Web 2 internet, etc.

On top of this, many post quantum encryption methods are more bandwidth intensive to encrypt each packet of data. Some estimates indicate that this could mean up to 10x more bandwidth is needed.

In a centralized network, this is manageable (but costly).

On the Bitcoin side? Decentralized and unregulated. You can’t force anyone to comply with quantum upgrades. On top of this, each node in the Bitcoin network will need to upgrade its hardware to handle post-quantum bandwidth needs.

That’s a big ask. I don’t know if the blockchain will survive.

Takeaway

While Bitcoin has had an incredible run, I am double downgrading my stance on IBIT from a strong buy to a hold. I believe that the easy gains have been booked, and the balance of factors is now really net neutral (or even cautious). At $114k per Bitcoin, chasing the rally from here is risky. Much of the bull case is already baked in, and I think rising risks are underappreciated.

Bitcoin’s long-term fundamentals remain intact only if they can figure out how to perfect encryption against quantum. Perfect timing of a cycle top is nearly impossible. There is a chance Bitcoin could spike to $130k before reversing course yet again. This hold rating means I’m not trying to time the top, but I am recognizing the fact that the number of risks is growing.

In the end, at these levels, I’d rather forgo some upside than risk getting caught in a sudden drop.

data.title: Carillon Eagle Mid Cap Growth Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview

Mid-cap stocks staged a strong recovery in the second quarter, rebounding from the broad drawdown at the beginning of the year. Among the style indexes, the Russell Midcap® Growth Index (up 18.20%) outperformed the Russell Midcap® Value Index (up 5.34%) by a notable degree. Within the growth index, utilities (up 65.66%) delivered a standout absolute return that led all other sectors by a wide margin.

Communication services (up 36.75%) also posted impressive results for the quarter. Information technology (up 26.03%) made the biggest contribution to overall returns as the sector in the index with the largest weight. Financials (up 18.22%) closely matched the growth index’s return, outperforming larger-weighted sectors such as industrials (up 17.42%), consumer discretionary (up 12.83%), and healthcare (up 12.57%). Real estate (up 8.63%), consumer staples (up 4.48%), and materials (up 2.06%) lagged but outpaced their value counterparts. Energy (down 9.65%) was the only sector both to post a loss and to underperform its value counterpart.

Top securities

Palantir Technologies (PLTR), a leader in artificial intelligence (AI) software and services to governmental organizations, reported robust results and provided a strong outlook, as both the U.S. military and NATO purchased large contracts. Palantir also has had success penetrating non-military agencies of the U.S. government, as well as the commercial (corporate) side of the market with AI use cases that deliver quantifiable added value through its bootcamp product demonstrations.

Axon Enterprise is a market-leading provider of next-generation law enforcement and security technology solutions. The company continues to post strong quarterly results, as it has achieved ongoing success with innovative new products that have garnered significant attention among both customers and investors. Axon remains intensely focused on the development of innovative solutions to address all aspects of law enforcement and has made significant strides in the emerging areas of drones and AI-enabled solutions. In addition, the company continues to expand its offerings outside of its traditional law enforcement area, and it has recently highlighted the potential opportunities that could lie ahead in these new markets.

Royal Caribbean Cruises operates a fleet of vacation cruise ships. The stock performed well as demand for cruising remained robust.

Quarterly results outpaced expectations and guidance was lifted. Royal Caribbean is benefitting from enhancements to its cruise offerings since the period before the COVID-19 pandemic, with more moderate price increases than other travel options.

Cloudflare provides software and services to keep internet traffic safe and secure. Investors have responded positively to the company’s continued business growth, driven by the adoption of newer, software-based tools that help enterprises secure their networks more effectively than outdated legacy hardware solutions. In addition, we believe Cloudflare’s emerging AI capabilities show strong potential and could serve as a meaningful growth catalyst heading into 2026.

Vistra is an integrated electricity and power generation company. As a result of increasing forecasts for future power demand growth, largely brought on by the rapid growth of artificial intelligence, the company’s shares have continued to climb on investors’ expectations for future power prices. A tailwind for the stock has been Vistra’s potential to announce future power purchase agreements (PPAs) with large technology companies to satisfy the outsized power requirements of their artificial intelligence endeavors.

Bottom securities

Baker Hughes (BKR) is a diversified energy technology and equipment company. Shares declined on concerns about what the recent downturn in oil prices would mean for global upstream activity going forward. Despite this, Baker Hughes continues to see healthy order trends within its Industrial & Energy Technology segment, as the company is experiencing a notable uptick in demand for its power-generation solutions. The company also remains well-positioned to capitalize on the continued buildout of liquefied natural gas (LNG) infrastructure across the globe.

Sarepta Therapeutics (SRPT) develops RNA-targeted medicines and gene therapies for rare neuromuscular diseases. The company reported quarterly revenue that exceeded expectations, but posted a larger than expected loss per share and revised its 2025 sales forecast downward. The stock sold off sharply after a second patient died from acute liver failure after being treated with Elevidys, its gene therapy for patients with Duchenne muscular dystrophy.

Okta (OKTA), a provider of cybersecurity software with a focus on identity and access, reported solid results for the quarter, but the outlook was below expectations, which was especially disappointing after a strong move higher in the stock. The challenging macroeconomic environment resulted in prospective corporate customers turning slightly more pessimistic, which, along with near-term uncertainty in the federal segment, resulted in muted guidance. However, the need for security software remains intact, and the company should continue to benefit from cross-selling new products, such as identity governance and privileged access management that provides higher security for C-level executives.

Ryan (RYAN) is a leading insurance broker specializing in excess and surplus lines (E&S) insurance. While first-quarter results exceeded expectations, the stock delivered lackluster results due to a slightly disappointing quarterly margin and investor concerns over a potentially challenging internal growth comparison in the second quarter. We view the margin miss as temporary and anticipate margin expansion throughout the remainder of the year and beyond. Additionally, while shorter-term property pricing pressures persist, we believe they will be outweighed over time by the outsized organic growth expected in the E&S market.

RH (RH) is a home furnishings retailer that designs, sells, and markets furniture, décor, textiles, and related products. The stock lagged driven by a combination of tariff pressures and weaker results. We were more concerned with the weaker business results, which marked a shift from the positive momentum the company had been building. A number of factors including consumer caution, higher interest rates, and overall housing weakness combined to nullify the strides RH has made in product and pricing strategies.
