Outlook

The second quarter proved to be one of the most turbulent environments for equity markets in recent memory. Driven by historically high levels of trade policy uncertainty, stocks suffered steep declines in April with most market averages entering bear market territory and investor sentiment surveys reaching extreme levels of bearishness. Markets sharply rebounded as tariffs were paused and appeared likely to be far less punitive than the draconian scenarios introduced on Liberation Day. Earnings season also served as a catalyst to help stocks recover their losses as the anticipated impact of tariffs in many cases turned out to be more manageable than initially feared. Additionally, many of the momentum stocks associated with the investment theme around AI surged as large hyperscalers either reiterated or raised their capital spending plans and provided strong evidence of continued strength in user adoption trends. Looking ahead, we believe the continued uncertainty on trade policy is likely to persist for the foreseeable future but believe the market, for now, appears willing to heavily discount the headlines associated with the current administration’s negotiating tactics. We expect inflationary data to remain relatively favorable, allowing the U.S. Federal Reserve to lower interest rates in the second half of the year. During the coming months, as the market shifts its valuation focus to 2026, we see a reasonable base case for solid earnings growth. Reduced trade and policy uncertainty, potential fiscal stimulus, and lower interest rates are the key assumptions supporting this constructive outlook. These conditions should foster a favorable macroeconomic environment and allow a broadening of the market beyond the AI investment theme. In this backdrop, we believe high-quality secular growth stocks are positioned to perform well.

Cyclicals

The outlook for the cyclical areas of the market appears broadly constructive but selectivity remains key. Within the energy sector, the outlook for natural gas prices appears favorable given rising LNG exports and higher electricity demand from the buildout of data centers. In contrast, oil prices appear likely to remain subdued as OPEC+ – the Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries, plus 10 other oil-producing countries, including Russia – shifts its strategy to win back market share from North American producers. The outlook for aerospace and defense continues to remain strong. Signs of improvement in manufacturing bottlenecks throughout the aerospace supply chain suggest that the largest aircraft manufacturers appear to be on track to ramp monthly production rates toward their targets. Defense companies should also get a boost from an increasingly tumultuous geopolitical landscape, alongside growing defense spending by European nations as they begin to meet their NATO commitments. Housing remains challenged as mortgage rates remain at levels that constrict affordability and restrain residential construction. The AI data center buildout remains a favorable tailwind for companies throughout the electrical equipment industry as utilities ramp up spending to meet rising demand for power generation. Finally, further resolution of trade policy uncertainty in coming months should help promote investment in U.S. manufacturing, and we believe factory automation spending will be a durable investment theme for the next several years.

Healthcare

The healthcare industry remains the largest sector in the United States, with total annual expenditures reaching $5 trillion and accounting for 17.7% of gross domestic product (GDP). This sector has been growing at an impressive rate of about 5% per year, driven by the aging population and the increasing prevalence of chronic conditions, which are responsible for 75% of healthcare costs. Most individuals over the age of 65 have two or more chronic conditions, and Western medicine often struggles to manage these effectively. Additionally, novel therapies are extending lifespans allowing people to live well into their 80s, but these therapies are expensive and significantly contribute to rising healthcare costs.

Despite the sector’s size and importance, healthcare has underperformed in recent years, generating lackluster returns compared with major benchmarks. The sector has been working off the excesses brought on by the COVID-19 pandemic. The massive deceleration in demand for healthcare products and services after 2021 led to a significant decline in stock prices and, in turn, caused the sector to generate very poor returns on an absolute and relative basis. The surge in initial public offerings (IPOs), particularly of biotechnology companies during 2020 and 2021, reduced the overall quality of drug candidates being developed, resulting in higher than normal negative clinical readouts and investor abandonment. This has caused volatility not only among biotech stocks, but also among healthcare stocks in general. Healthcare is a highly regulated industry, overseen by agencies such as the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services, the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services, the Food and Drug Administration, and the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention. Regulatory uncertainty, especially with new directors and commissioners being installed, has further deterred investors. Add to that the uncertainty over the provisions in the “One Big Beautiful Bill” that could negatively impact healthcare stocks, creating additional angst among investors.

There are, however, reasons for optimism. Recent mergers and acquisition (M&A) activity has picked up, though it is still below normal levels. Much of the M&A activity has been focused on medical device and biotechnology companies, where larger acquirers are looking to smaller, innovative companies to provide them with new products to enhance their own portfolios and market positions. From a valuation perspective, healthcare is generally cheaper than most sectors currently. Once investors are comfortable with the impact of tariffs, and other policy and regulatory overhangs, interest in healthcare stocks could see a resurgence. Until then, larger active bets are being placed on predictable, U.S.-centric businesses like major distributors, which have steady margins and generate substantial free cash flow.

Interest from generalist investors is expected to continue to trend positively into the second half of the year, following the potential passage of the One Big Beautiful Bill along with the finalization of annual reimbursement schedules by the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services.

Technology

Markets entered the second quarter with renewed volatility, as the announcement of sweeping tariffs in early April triggered a sharp sell-off in equities, particularly in hardware and semiconductor stocks. In contrast, software companies proved to be more resilient, with their recurring revenue models and lower exposure to global supply chains helping to offset the blow. Investor sentiment quickly deteriorated amid fears of a prolonged trade conflict and its potential impact on corporate earnings.

However, those concerns have thus far proven largely overdone. As the quarter progressed, greater clarity from the administration on trade negotiations, along with reassuring commentary and results during earnings season, helped ease fears and sparked a strong rebound in stocks. Despite lingering uncertainty, inflation expectations have remained stable, and the prospect of further trade deals have provided additional support for risk assets. In this environment, technology companies with strong balance sheets and consistent cash flow generation continue to stand out as potential investment opportunities. The current administration is also expected to create a more constructive backdrop for a rebound in mergers and acquisitions, as well as a potential revival in IPO activity. Within the technology sector, we continue to identify durable, well-capitalized companies that we believe are poised to benefit from long-term secular trends such as cloud computing, artificial intelligence, mobility and telecommunications infrastructure, digital payments, the Internet of Things, smart homes, industrial automation, security software, e-gaming, and alternative energy.

Financials

The outlook for the financials sector is constructive as economic growth has stayed resilient and fears about growth have thus far been overblown. Interest rates have settled somewhat from the volatility seen earlier in the year. In this environment, we see the most interesting opportunities outside of the typical banking space. Areas we remain excited about include boutique advisory firms poised to benefit from an increase in M&A activity that will accompany the eventual decrease in interest rates. In addition, we see potential opportunities in the alternative asset space as those products continue to grow and become available in the wealth management and retail channels. Lastly, we see what we regard as distinct opportunities within the transaction processor space.

The consumer

The continued, although slowing, moderation in inflation and thus-far steady job market continue to provide support to the consumer. Tariffs have injected some additional uncertainty into the consumer outlook although thus far the overall impacts appear manageable. Against this backdrop, we find a few different dynamics at play:

  • Consumers continue to prioritize travel and experiences,
  • Consumers remain interested in overall health and wellness, and
  • Consumers continue to be quite discerning about where they want to spend their discretionary funds.

Some areas we remain excited about include companies offering exclusive experiences, ranging from cruise vacations to spa treatments while on cruises to trips to amusement parks. We are excited that online sports betting continues to grow with a number of providers approaching profitability. Lastly, we are excited about individual retailers offering distinctive products that we believe can weather the tariff storm.

Risk Considerations:

Investments in mid-cap and small-cap companies generally involve greater risks than investing in larger capitalization companies. Mid-cap companies often have narrower commercial markets, more limited managerial and financial resources, and more volatile trading than larger, more established companies.

Growth companies are expected to increase their earnings at a certain rate. When these expectations are not met, investors may punish the stocks excessively, even if earnings showed an absolute increase. Growth company stocks also typically lack the dividend yield that can cushion stock prices in market downturns. The companies engaged in the technology industry are subject to fierce competition, and their products and services may be subject to rapid obsolescence. The values of these companies tend to fluctuate sharply.

Definitions

Absolute return is the return that an asset achieves over a specified period of time. A relative return is the return an asset achieves compared to a benchmark indicator over a specific period.

Alternative assets, also known as alternative investments, refer to any investments or asset classes that fall outside the traditional asset classes of stocks, bonds, or cash. They can include hedge funds, private equity, private debt, venture capital, managed futures, derivatives contracts, commodities, cryptocurrency, art and antiques, and, in some cases, real estate. Alternative assets are often less regulated and less liquid than traditional asset classes.

Artificial intelligence (AI) is technology that enables computers and machines to simulate human learning, comprehension, problem solving, decision making, creativity and autonomy.

Capital expenditures, or capex, are monies used by a company to buy, improve, or maintain assets such as real estate, facilities, technology, or equipment, and may include new projects or investments.

A clinical readout in healthcare is the publication or release of the results from a clinical trial or study of a new medicine, procedure, or healthcare product.

Comparables, often shortened to comps, carries different meanings depending on the industry and context, but generally entails a comparison of financial metrics – often for two different time periods – or other factors to quantify performance or determine valuation.

Cyclical stocks have prices influenced by macroeconomic changes in the economy and are known for following the economy as it cycles through expansion, peak, recession, and recovery.

A drawdown is a decline in the returns of a security or group of securities, as measured over a period from the peak of returns to their trough.

Excess and surplus lines (E&S) insurance, also known as surplus lines insurance, offers protection against financial risks that are too great or too uncommon for a regular insurance company to take on.

Fiscal policy refers to the tax collection and spending a government uses to influence its country’s economy.

Generalist investors typically invest in a wider range of industries, sectors, or other areas of the market than specialist investors with a more narrow focus.

Gross domestic product (GDP) is the total value of goods and services provided in an economy during a specified period, often one year.

Growth investing focuses on companies expected to grow at an above-average rate compared to their industry or the market.

Guidance refers statements from the managers of publicly traded companies about their expectations for near-term profits or losses.

Hyperscaler refers to the largest cloud computing providers that can provide massive amounts of computing resources and storage at enterprise scale.

Liberation Day is a term used by President Donald Trump to refer to April 2, 2025, when he announced a wide range of unexpectedly high tariffs on many U.S. trading partners, triggering a selloff of risk assets.

Macroeconomic refers to the branch of economics that seeks to understand the interactions between the markets, businesses, governments, and consumers that make up an entire economy.

Margin expansion refers to a rise in the percentage of revenue that a company earns as profit.

Market capitalization, or market cap, refers to the total dollar market value of a company’s outstanding shares of stock.

A miss is when a company’s reported earnings or other business results fall short of pre-release estimates or are worse than the expectations of analysts and others who follow the company’s stock.

Momentum stocks benefit from the continuance of an existing and often accelerating market trend. In momentum trading, investors buy securities that are already rising and look to sell them when they look to have peaked.

NATO, or the North Atlantic Treaty Organization, is a political and military alliance of 32 countries from Europe and North America that is organized to guarantee their security and cooperation.

The One, Big, Beautiful Bill Act was passed by Congress in July 2025 to make permanent tax cuts created by the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act of 2017, as well as increase the U.S. debt ceiling by $5 trillion, change a wide range of other aspects of federal tax policy, make changes to health insurance legislation, phase out or reduce credits for clean energy production or use, and remove tax benefits for illegal immigrants, among other things.

OPEC+ consists of OPEC, plus 10 other oil-producing countries (in order of production): Russia, Mexico, Kazakhstan, Oman, Azerbaijan, Malaysia, Bahrain, South Sudan, Brunei, and Sudan. OPEC+ was established in 2016 as rising U.S. shale oil production caused prices to fall dramatically. The Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries, or OPEC, was founded in 1960 and is a permanent organization of 13 oil-exporting developing nations that coordinates the petroleum policies of its member countries, which are Iran, Iraq, Kuwait, Saudi Arabia, Venezuela, Libya, the United Arab Emirates, Algeria, Nigeria, Gabon, Angola, Equatorial Guinea, and Congo.

Organic growth is the growth that companies achieve through using their own internal resources to increase production or sales. It does not include growth or revenues from mergers, acquisitions, or other external methods of adding to sales or profits.

Overhang occurs when investors wait for an expected event or set of circumstances to play out before they are willing to buy a stock.

Power purchase agreements (PPAs) are arrangements in which third-party developers install, own, and operate energy systems on customers’ properties. These agreements are most commonly used for renewable energy systems, and customers purchase the electric output of these systems for a predetermined period.

Secular stocks are characterized by having consistent earnings over the long term constant regardless of other trends in the market. Secular companies often have a primary business related to consumer staples most households consistently use whether the larger economy is good or bad.

Secular trends are large-scale and ongoing changes in economies and societies that have the potential to drive broad and lasting economic, technological, social or other kinds of changes.

Tailwind is a term used to describe events or market forces that exert a positive influence on an investment’s performance.

Upstream expenditures for oil and natural gas production activities include property acquisition, exploration and development, and production.

Exploration and development includes searching for and developing the facilities and infrastructure to produce reserves. Production includes extracting oil and natural gas from the ground once the field has been developed. Property acquisition includes buying proved and unproved oil and natural gas reserves.

Value investing is an investment strategy that involves picking stocks that appear to be trading for less than their intrinsic or book value.

Indices

The Russell Midcap® Growth Index, the Fund’s benchmark index, measures the performance of those Russell Midcap companies with higher price-to-book ratios and higher forecasted growth values. The stocks are also members of the Russell 1000 Growth® Index.

The Russell Midcap® Value Index measures the performance of those Russell Midcap companies with lower price-to-book ratios and lower forecasted growth values. The stocks are also members of the Russell 1000 Value® Index.

data.title: Gold Fields Limited: Run Ahead Of Itself
data.description:
Intro

Gold Fields Limited (GFI), founded way back in 1887 by Cecil Rhodes and Charles Rudd in South Africa as Gold Fields of South Africa Limited, is a senior gold mining company headquartered in Johannesburg. Despite having its HQ there, South Africa indeed plays only a minor role when talking about its mining portfolio.

The company currently operates nine mines across South Africa, Ghana, Australia, Peru, and Chile. From the screenshot below, we can see that Australia accounts for roughly half of GFI's total gold production, followed by Ghana (32%), the home country South Africa (13%), and Peru (9%).

In August 2024, Gold Fields acquired Osisko Mining for C$2.16 billion, or roughly $1.5 billion, gaining full control of the promising Windfall Project in Québec, Canada (one of the largest and highest-grade gold development projects globally), to strengthen its already well diversified portfolio with another long-life asset.

In May 2025, the next deal was announced. Gold Fields acquired Gold Road Resources for a sweetened offer of A$3.7 billion ($2.4 billion) after the initial $2.1 billion were rejected. This acquisition allows for full ownership of the Gruyere gold mine in Western Australia, where GFI has already been the operator. In that sense, this strategic move strengthens GFI's core Australian portfolio in one of the most stable mining jurisdictions.

The story of GFI is a real success. However, everything has a price. And in this regard, GFI who long has been a laggard behind its bigger peers in terms of valuation, has outpaced everybody, including itself. That's why I am rating GFI a cautious sell, as expectations seem to be very high at this stage.

Strong performance, good outlook

Gold Fields just today reported a fresh set of results. In the first half of 2025, GFI showed a strong performance. Revenues surged 64% to $3.48 billion, up from $2.12 billion in the first half of 2024. This was driven by a 17% increase in gold-equivalent ounces sold and a 40% higher gold price. It is good to see that it was not only the higher gold price as an external force that lifted sales.

Further, as can be seen below, GFI produced 1,136,000 ounces of gold on an attributable basis (up 24% from 918,000 ounces), with significant contributions from Salares Norte’s ramp-up in Chile.

When looking at production on a mine-level, Tarkwa (Ghana) led with 270,000 ounces (down 4% from 281,000 in H1 2024), followed by St Ives (Australia) at 197,000 ounces (up 38% from 143,000), Gruyere (Australia) at 150,000 ounces (up 15% from 130,000), Granny Smith (Australia) at 141,000 ounces (up 5% from 134,000), South Deep (South Africa) at 142,000 ounces (up 14% from 125,000), Cerro Corona (Peru) at 94,000 gold-equivalent ounces (down 10% from 104,000), Damang (Ghana) at 77,000 ounces (down 8% from 84,000), and Salares Norte (Chile) at 65,000 gold-equivalent ounces (up 225% from 20,000). The latter was boosted by significant progress in Q2 2025, particularly the ramp-up of operations following winter-related delays.

All-in sustaining costs (AISC) rose to $1,739 per ounce in the second quarter (up 7% from $1,625 per ounce a year earlier), reflecting mining inflation. On a half-year basis, AISC indeed fell from $1,745 per ounce to $1,682.

Net income soared disproportionately by 163% to $1.06 billion in the first half of 2025, up from $402 million in the first half of 2024. On a per share basis, EPS came in at $1.15, from $0.43 per share. Results were, like sales, boosted by higher volumes and gold prices, though offset by increased costs.

Adjusted free cash flow turned positive at $952 million in the first half of 2025, compared to a $58 million outflow in the first half of 2024. This strong improvement was driven by better operating cash flows, though slightly reduced by increased capital spending.

Additionally, Gold Fields increased its interim dividend to 700 ZAR cents per share for H1 2025, up a staggering 133% from 300 ZAR cents per share in H1 2024, as a result of the strong financial performance and in accordance with its dividend policy to distribute between 30 and 45 percent of normalized earnings. This equates to approximately $0.39 per share at the current exchange rate. On an annualized basis (paid bi-annually), the yield is only about 2.7% at the current share price.

The outlook foresees gold production to reach 2.39–2.45 million ounces (up from 2.33 million ounces in 2024), with AISC projected at $1,650–$1,730 per ounce (up from $1,407 per ounce), driven by higher output from Salares Norte and sustained high gold prices.

Windfall update

In mid-2025, Gold Fields’ Windfall Project advanced by submitting environmental documents and progressing engineering. The project team was strengthened, and discussions with the Cree First Nation intensified.

Gold Fields is negotiating with the Cree First Nation to agree on how the Windfall Project can sustainably benefit their community with jobs and development.

A key investment decision is targeted for early 2026. If everything goes well, the processing plant is set to start production in 2028, contributing 300,000 ounces of gold annually at lower-than-average costs.

Balance Sheet – bullet proof

Gold Fields’ net debt-to-adjusted EBITDA ratio improved to 0.37 at June 30, 2025 (from 0.53 at June 30, 2024). In total figures, Gold Fields has $1.1 billion in cash vs. gross financial debt of $2.1 billion, with no near-term maturities.

The acquisition of Gold Road Resources, an all-cash deal, will be financed through cash on hand, free cash flow, and debt. But I do not view this as a risky maneuver. The finances are solid.

Valuation – outshining the competition

When looking at the performance of the stock, we can see that GFI has massively outcompeted its bigger peers Newmont (NEM), Barrick Mining (B), and Agnico Eagle Mines (AEM) over the last ten years.

Notably, GFI during the first 4-5 years was lagging, then on par and over the last three years really started to show massive torque.

Generating more than 800% over the last rolling decade, and 250% over the last three years, was not just based on fundamentals. GFI stock saw a significant multiple expansion, which for my taste makes the stock now look dangerously valued.

Using the price to tangible book value – which is far from a perfect tool, but it is directionally working – we can see that Gold Fields (blue line) here too, first was lagging, but then suddenly went into overdrive.

The result is that GFI stock now spots a P/TBV of a staggering 5.3x according to TIKR Terminal. Looking into the latest balance sheet, we can see $6.2 billion in equity. In relation to a market cap of $27.4 billion, this is still 4.4x.

Way ahead of the competition, which I consider to be partly of higher quality, especially Agnico-Eagle Mines, which operates primarily in safer Tier-1 jurisdictions, and at lower AISC.

I have no plausible arguments for the valuation premium.

As a reminder, less safe Tier-2 and lower jurisdictions can come up with surprising developments. We don't need to look any further than at Gold Fields' Ghanaian Damang mine, which was denied a lease extension.

With only about half the portfolio in a Tier-1 jurisdiction, GFI should rather be valued at a modest discount, not at a premium. This makes the stock a sell for me, even though on an operational level it is delivering.

Risks to my Thesis

A higher gold price would likely lift the stock higher, making my sell rating obsolete.

Even though I am currently not expecting it, it is possible that one of the big tickets might come up with a bid to acquire Gold Fields. The larger mining companies become, the more they rely on deals to replenish their reserves and resources. With an average estimated reserve life of 22 years, not counting resources, good arguments are on the table (respectively under the ground).

Conclusion

Gold Fields stock has shown a strong performance, especially over the last three years since the price of gold took off. However, things went too fast. Gold Fields is now valued the highest among its peer group, despite having riskier assets with only about half of gold production coming from Australia. This makes GFI stock a sell for me, as I do see enough upside to justify such a price tag.

data.title: Palantir: Nothing Like The Dot-Com Bubble
data.description:
Introduction & Investment Thesis

When I last wrote about Palantir (NASDAQ:PLTR) (NEOE:PLTR:CA), I rated the stock a “buy,” as I believed that Palantir’s AIP (Artificial Intelligence Platform) is looking similarly positioned to how NVIDIA’s (NVDA) CUDA was at the start of the AI Revolution.

Since then, the company reported its Q2 FY25 earnings, which left investors speechless, as it not only managed to keep its revenue acceleration trend alive for 8 consecutive quarters in a row but also scored 94% in its Rule of 40 score, which is unmatched by any enterprise software company at the moment.

However, last week, after Sam Altman said that he believed that AI may be in a bubble, the stock shed over 15% of its market cap after reaching an all-time high price of $190 per share.

With fears of an AI bubble creeping back into markets again, I wanted to first emphasize that AI capex is far from reaching a peak, especially as spending shifts from training to inference with enterprises deploying AI at scale.

With a growing inference TAM (total addressable market), Palantir’s AIP becomes indispensable as its AI-native control layer embeds itself across the entire operating stack of a company, takes real-time data across systems, turns it into actionable simulations across business cases, and generates outsized ROI from its customers.

Second, while parallels to the dotcom bust are often drawn when talking about a potential AI bubble, I want to point out that today’s tech companies are much better grounded compared to the dotcom era, with rock-solid balance sheets and growing revenue and profitability.

Sure, Palantir’s forward valuation is the talk of the AI bear villa, but know that the company has been innovating while growing its profitability. In fact, since the beginning of the year, the management’s growth projection for its US Commercial Revenue has risen from 54% to 85% for the full year, while expecting greater margins.

While there are risks in the AI investment landscape, especially with growing capex depreciation expenses, which could hurt margins, along with a growing number of credit investors pouring billions of dollars into artificial intelligence investments lately, I believe there is no imminent danger as long as AI companies, such as Palantir and others, continue to meet and exceed earnings expectations, like they did in Q2.

With the tailwind of AI spending shifting to inference in the coming years, along with an accommodative monetary policy, it should support Palantir’s valuation multiple, especially as its forward revenue and earnings estimates are getting revised higher.

As a result, I will reiterate my “buy” rating on the stock with a price target of $190 per share at the moment.

Palantir’s Q2 left investors speechless (once again)

Palantir reported its Q2 FY25 earnings in early August, where it left investors spellbound once again, with revenues now accelerating for 8 consecutive quarters, growing 48% YoY to $1.003B, exceeding management’s expectations by 7%.

During the quarter, both its Commercial and Government Revenue segments accelerated from the previous quarter, growing 47% and 49% YoY, respectively, to $451M and $553M, respectively.

When it comes to Palantir’s Commercial Revenue, it is the US region that is responsible for contributing close to 68% of the segment’s revenue, growing at an eye-watering rate of 93% YoY, up from 71% in the previous quarter, with its US Commercial Customer count showing no signs of slowdown, growing 64% YoY to 485.

This is driven by none other than Palantir’s AIP, which Palantir’s CTO, Shyam Sankar, described as follows:

“AIP isn't just software our customers use, it's software, our customers are building their software on," and highlighted accelerating adoption among frontline workers and rapid deployment of AI-driven solutions in U.S. industries.”

If you remember, in my previous post, I compared the indispensability of Palantir’s AIP in the second wave of the AI Revolution to the role Nvidia’s CUDA played in the first wave of the cycle.

Well, I fully stand by what I said earlier.

You can see it with the rate at which Palantir is closing its US Commercial deals over $1M and $5M in value, with US Commercial Total Contract Value growing at an accelerated rate of 222% YoY and the net dollar retention rate expanding sequentially to 128%, driven both by the expansion at existing customers and the acquisition of new customers.

Meanwhile, if you are thinking that Palantir is managing to grow at these rates while sacrificing profitability, you are wrong.

In my previous posts on Palantir, I had discussed that the company’s go-to-market model is centered on AIP Bootcamps and AIPCon to unlock potential AI use cases for existing and new clients instead of hoarding salespeople.

This model has allowed them to capture phenomenal economies of scale, where its adjusted operating margin beat expectations by 300 basis points at 46%, growing both on a sequential and year-over-year basis, as can be seen below.

Not only that, but Palantir’s Rule of 40 score continued to edge higher, currently sitting at 94% and unmatched by any other enterprise software company with >$1B in TTM (Trailing Twelve Months) Revenue.

Moving forward, Palantir’s revenue growth isn’t expected to slow down anytime soon, despite facing incredibly tough comps. For Q3, management expects revenue of $1.085B (in the midpoint), which would mark the 9th consecutive quarter of revenue acceleration at 50% YoY growth, which represents a year-over-year growth rate of close to 50% with a similar adjusted operating margin of 46%.

For the full year, the management now expects to generate total revenues of roughly $4.146B, with US Commercial Revenue projected to grow 85% and an adjusted operating margin of over 46%.

Just to give you a picture of the momentum of AIP, Palantir management has revised their full-year revenue guidance by over 10% since the beginning of the year, while expectations for the growth rate in the company's US Commercial revenue have expanded by 3100 basis points during this period of time (from 54% at the start of the year to 85% as of the current quarter).

Inference is about to explode regardless of Sam Altman’s comments

However, since Palantir stock created an all-time high of $190 per share, it has shed more than 15% of its market cap over the last week.

This took place after OpenAI CEO Sam Altman said that the artificial intelligence market may be in a bubble. This is what he said below.

“When bubbles happen, smart people get overexcited about a kernel of truth. Are we in a phase where investors as a whole are overexcited about AI? My opinion is yes. Is AI the most important thing to happen in a very long time? My opinion is also yes.”

Unfortunately, since markets often have a very difficult time connecting dots, it quickly discounted the news from last month, where Sam Altman said that he plans to deploy 1M GPUs by 2025, while eyeing a 100-fold increase to 100M GPUs for AGI advancement.

While the path to 100M GPUs would require massive capital infusions of up to $3T or more, it makes one thing very clear. That is, the demand for AI compute isn’t going away anytime soon, which OpenAI’s CFO Sarah Friar further reiterated in a CNBC interview last week.

As a result, I believe the AI capex boom is far from reaching its peak.

According to Goldman Sachs, hyperscaler capex will increase sharply through 2027, with capex projected to be $1.15T from 2025 through 2027, more than double the $477B spent from 2022 through 2024.

In the most recent quarterly earnings, the four largest hyperscalers, which include Amazon (AMZN), Microsoft (MSFT), Alphabet (GOOG), and Meta (META), pointed out that their capex investments will exceed their prior estimates as compute capacity continues to remain constrained while the backlog of AI workloads continues to grow.

If you still need further evidence of the AI boom, check out the explosion in token usage across these companies, where Microsoft reported a 7x YoY growth rate in tokens served and processed by Foundry APIs, while Alphabet has doubled the number of tokens processed since May of this year.

The way I see it, this only means one thing.

The demand for inference is about to explode, like I have discussed several times in my previous posts on Palantir.

As Shay Boloor, Chief Market Strategist at Futurum Equities, pointed out in this Twitter post recently,

“This isn’t just about training LLMs. It’s about every enterprise app going AI-native. Every workflow, every vertical, and every user interface will need low-latency inference, secure data pipelines, and orchestration layers that simply don’t run on legacy infrastructure. It runs in the cloud—and it runs on clouds built for AI. So no, I don’t buy the “capex cliff” story. Not when enterprises are just beginning to fine-tune models and deploy agents into production.”

In other words, this is the time when Palantir’s AIP will increasingly become an indispensable layer in the AI operating stack, as it embeds itself across the entire operating stack of a company, takes real-time data across systems, turns it into actual simulations across business cases, and generates outsized ROI for their customers.

Here is the thing. We all may still be underestimating the amount of inference compute we need with Agentic AI, where enterprises deploy AI at scale across use cases.

As a result, you can see the magnitude of upward revisions in forward revenue that Palantir has received over the last 6 months (with no signs of slowdown).

How big is the risk of an AI bubble popping Palantir’s valuation?

Whenever the topic of the AI bubble emerges, investors quickly start to draw parallels between the current investment frenzy in artificial intelligence and the dot-com bubble in the late 1990s.

Since there is already plenty of available content on the dot-com bubble on the internet, I won’t repeat the mechanics of how the bubble and burst transpired in that era.

Instead, I am going to draw your attention to this chart below from Apollo Management’s Torsten Slok, where he says that the stock market’s expectations are even more unrealistic today, with 12-month forward price-to-earnings ratios now exceeding the peak of the dot-com bubble.

But here is the thing.

Of the top 10 companies by market capitalization today, eight are tech or communications behemoths. This contrasts with only five out of the 10 biggest companies that were tech firms back in 1999.

On top of that, the top 10 companies’ footprint in the S&P 500 is significantly higher today than it was in 1999, reflecting the fact that technology plays a much bigger role in the US economy today than it did at the turn of the millennium.

In the meantime, Palantir’s forward price-to-earnings multiple of 250 is not something to take lightly.

However, if we take the broader picture into account, the S&P 500 and Nasdaq are currently trading around 22 and 28.5 times forward earnings, compared with the dot-com peaks of 24.5 and over 70 times, respectively.

Plus, I firmly believe that the 1990s dot-com bubble was a lesson in unsustainable business models, where the bubble was characterized by a frenzy of public offerings, where stock prices of anything associated with a dot-com address skyrocketed, fueled by the fear of missing out.

That is not the case today. At least, not yet.

On the contrary, today’s top megacap tech companies trade at more balanced forward price-to-earnings multiples in my view, as there has been a shift away from “growth at all costs” to “growing profitably,” especially with interest rates elevated since 2022. On top of that, these industry giants have incredibly strong balance sheets, with historic levels of cash flows that are driving their AI investments.

Palantir is no different either.

The company has been innovating more by spending less, while their cash from operations has grown over 270% YoY with a margin expansion (as a percentage of revenue) of 3300 basis points to 54%.

That is not to say that Palantir doesn't face any risks of its valuation multiple contracting from current levels should fears of an AI bubble popping start to grow in the markets.

First, the growth in AI capex comes with a large depreciation bill that can squeeze cash flows and margins, especially if revenues fail to grow fast enough.

While Morgan Stanley sees AI revenues surpassing $1T by 2028, a jump from $45B in 2024, we are talking about a jump of over 20-fold, which is ambitious in my opinion.

Second, we are also starting to see credit investors pouring billions of dollars into artificial intelligence investments, with JPMorgan Chase and Mitsubishi UFJ Financial Group leading the sale of more than a $22B loan to support Vantage Data Center’s plan to build a massive data center campus, while Meta Platforms is getting $29B from PIMCO and Blue Owl Capital for a massive data center in Louisiana.

Not to mention that OpenAI will need trillions of dollars over time to spend on the infrastructure required to develop and run artificial intelligence services.

During this period of time, if revenues and earnings fail to impress investors, we will likely see a short-term cliff in AI capex spending, which would lead to multiple contractions in the AI investment landscape.

But as long as companies post stronger than expected revenue and earnings growth, like they did in Q2, the AI party will likely continue, despite fears of an AI bubble creeping into markets from time to time.

Plus, with the tailwinds of AI spending from training to inference, along with an accommodative monetary policy environment, I don’t see any imminent danger of a valuation contraction in the AI landscape. In other words, the current bull trajectory can continue to support Palantir’s valuation multiple for longer.

Revisiting my valuation

Looking forward, taking into account management’s full-year FY25 revenue projections, followed by consensus estimates for the two years after that, we arrive at a total estimated revenue of $7.5B by FY27.

Meanwhile, assuming that Palantir can keep its gross margins anchored at 82% while its non-GAAP operating expenses normalize to a growth rate of roughly 7% from next year onwards, it should be able to generate an adjusted operating income of $4.4B by FY27, with a margin of 59%, 1300 basis points higher than what is projected for FY25.

In other words, its adjusted operating income will grow at a compounded annual growth rate of 50% between FY25 and FY27.

Taking into account the S&P 500’s forward price-to-earnings ratio in FY25 at 24.5, with a projected earnings growth rate of 9%, I believe Palantir could fetch a multiple of 5.5 times that given the growth rate of its forward earnings growth rate, which would translate to a price target of $190 per share, representing an upside of 21% from its current levels.

My final verdict and conclusions

Palantir’s valuation is elevated, no doubt.

While I would not recommend going overweight on Palantir at these levels, especially if you are initiating a new position, I believe its valuation will remain supported as long as Palantir and companies in the AI investment landscape continue to meet investor expectations, despite risks of capex depreciation expenses and a growing number of credit investors pouring billions of dollars into the artificial intelligence investments.

As a result, I will reiterate my “buy” rating on the stock with a price target of $190, representing an upside of 20% from its current levels.

data.title: XLU: Utility Dividend Spread To Treasuries Is Too Low, Creating Overvaluation Risk
data.description:
In March, I upgraded my outlook on The Utilities Select Sector SPDR® Fund ETF (NYSEARCA:XLU), in "Potential Against Slowing Economy And Resurgent Inflation." My view was that utility stocks had similar bullish fundamentals to gold due to exposure to real physical assets with cash flow that should rise with inflation. Utility valuations are high, well above historically normal levels, but reasonable compared to the price of gold (discounted for real interest rates).

XLU has had decent returns since then, with a total return of 10.4% with 9.6% in appreciation. Year-to-date, XLU is the third-best-performing sector ETF with a 13.7% return, just behind Industrials (XLI) and Communications (XLC). In my view, much of XLU's strong performance is indirectly related to gold, both benefiting from defensive inflationary exposure. Despite higher real interest rates, both gold and utilities have risen since late 2023. Unfortunately, gold's momentum has slowed since April, potentially indicating that XLU is peaking. See below:

In my view, XLU's risk profile has increased due to gold's slowing momentum. I upgraded my outlook because gold was defying its typical negative correlation with real interest rates, meaning real assets could be "fairly valued" at very low yields as long as those yields are expected to keep up with inflation.

That said, XLU's TTM yield is now a meager 2.65%. Investors can earn ~1.3% over inflation through inflation-indexed Treasury bonds. This very low spread, combined with less "monetary devaluation" premium (implied by gold), may cause XLU to fall. Further, although most data still points to a rise in inflation, that has become less likely over the past five months.

Utility EPS Unlikely To Rise Above Inflation

I am among the fewer analysts who look at XLU and most major utility companies as primarily gold proxies. This relationship stems from the fact that gold and utility stocks have similar inflation and interest rate exposures. Higher inflation expectations tend to benefit them, while higher interest rates, particularly adjusted for expected inflation, usually cause them to devalue. The past two years have been very abnormal in that interest rates are high compared to inflation, yet gold and utilities have risen.

In the previous article, I traced this to increased risk of acceleration in inflation due to government financing pressures over the next decade (accelerating debt/deficit spending on interest). I expect the Federal Reserve to shift too far in a dovish direction, not because of unemployment or inflation, but to lower the government's financing debt burden. Officially, the recent change in tone toward more rate cuts is not due to pressure from Trump, yet, this would seem an unwise choice given unemployment is officially low and the inflation outlook has risen:

Now, as I've discussed in many articles, there are early signs of an economic slowdown. The Fed's change is not necessarily unreasonable, just odd compared to its positive economic statements. This includes the ongoing economic decline in much of the manufacturing sector, falling hiring activity, and flatter real median income growth. I see the recent decision to cut rates as an indication that the Federal Reserve may be moving in a dovish direction that eventually results in a larger increase in inflation. In general, XLU's dividend, electricity prices, and the consumer price index are closely tied:

Of course, there is also a growth narrative surrounding utility companies. US power demand has hardly grown over the past ~25 years, but is now rising and is expected to double by the late 2040s due to increased needs from data centers and an end to efficiency-saving power innovations (being the main reason for the lack of demand growth).

Although the power growth story is not unimportant and is critical for a handful of utility companies, I do not expect it will drive as much EPS growth as many expect. Regulated utilities are usually subject to return-on-equity caps to determine power rates, often around 9%. They do not operate in a free market and are basically monopolies with nearly guaranteed and predictable profits (excluding legal and regulatory risks). Rising power demand is driving utility capital expenditures higher, which is usually financed through debt or equity sales, limiting the direct EPS benefit to investors. Again, from the chart above, it is clear that XLU's dividend is largely a function of inflation and power prices.

Utilities also face capital costs from aged infrastructure. Most transformers, circuit breakers, and transmission lines in the US are closer to the end of their expected life. Thus, utilities have more power outages and may be more exposed to legal risks related to a lack of maintenance. PG&E (PCG) and Hawaii Electric (HE) are two notable examples of this issue.

In theory, utility companies can lobby to allow higher rates to finance much-needed CapEx projects. However, surveys show nearly three-quarters of Americans are upset about rising utility bills. More recently, we've seen Democrat politicians focus on this issue, with some looking to create legislation that may reduce ROE caps from (in this case) 9.7% to 4%. Though it is unclear if this proposal will go anywhere, it would be disastrous for XLU if such were adopted at a large scale.

This issue is somewhat bipartisan, with Trump promising to reduce electricity bills by 50% in his campaign. Utility companies are usually among the top lobby spenders in state and local politics. As more voters become concerned about rates, numerous states have recently banned utilities from using customer money for lobbying.

I think the idea that most utility companies will see EPS growth due to rising power demand is a bit misguided. It is true in some specific instances, but it is far truer for companies like Oklo (OKLO) that are creating technologies that may be sold to utilities to produce power. Very high capital investment needs for growth and for improving aged infrastructure may, in my opinion, result in dilution that offsets higher revenues.

Further, I think utility investors underappreciate the growing political backlash surrounding utility profits. If enough politicians agreed (which may be unlikely to begin with), they could act to dramatically reduce the profits (and therefore dividends) of XLU's constituents. Since this is one of the few issues with bipartisan consensus (among voters), I expect regulatory pushback will lower utility profit growth over the coming years.

XLU is Significantly Overvalued to Real Rates

XLU's dividend is akin to a "real interest rate", and is correlated to the real rate paid on shorter-term Treasuries. Both have dividends that should keep up with inflation. However, XLU's "premium" compared to Treasuries is extremely thin at just 1.37%, down from 3-4% during most of the post-2008 period:

I find this a tricky issue. As I explained in my last XLU article, if we assume that the US Federal Reserve will eventually pursue excessively dovish policies in order to push inflation higher and alleviate Treasury debt burdens, it makes sense for investors to own nearly any physical asset that should keep up with inflation. As an extreme example, let's say the US has hyperinflation where savings become worthless, we can expect costs and revenues in utilities to "pass through," indexing profits to inflation (specifically, power prices).

In my opinion, it is more likely that we see the Federal Reserve quietly raise the target rate from 2% to 3% to 4%, allowing the tax base to grow faster than the debt. Arguably, gold and equity valuations (particularly utilities) have risen as they price in this potential, which seems more inevitable after the last Fed meeting.

Still, XLU's tiny yield gives me pause, particularly with gold entering a trading range. In the short term, I do not expect the US government to pursue extreme financing measures. The debt is a ~10-year issue, not an immediate one, yet gold has acted as though it is more short-term.

Though I think inflation will average high over the next decade, there is a decent argument for short-term deflation. The US is not in a recession, but there are early signals of one (mainly hiring and productivity slowdowns). Home prices are now falling due to supply growth. Though tariffs "should" increase inflation, higher goods costs may be deflationary if companies are unable to pass costs forward.

In a recession, I expect temporary deflation followed by overly dovish Fed policies, leading to even higher long-term inflation. Basically, I expect what we saw in 2020 (in the monetary system) on a larger scale. Utilities would likely face less pressure from this than all other equity sectors, but it is bearish nonetheless.

The Bottom Line

My outlook on XLU is downgraded to slightly bearish from neutral. I became more neutral five months ago due to rate/inflation tailwinds implied by gold's rally, but that is no longer a supportive factor. Indeed, though I remain invested in precious metals, I think gold may be overvalued on a short-term basis, with limited upside until/unless QE restarts. My view on XLU is the same, but with a more negative outlook because of regulatory risks that may cut ROE and increase external financing needs for CapEx.

Investors who want to invest in the power demand growth narrative are likely better off in companies that create such capital. This includes nuclear, natural gas, and perhaps renewables (depending on the next election outcome). Brookfield Infrastructure Partners (BIP) is one of my favorite higher-yield equities within that segment.

data.title: RENK Group: Slight Outperformance After Q2, Still Expensive
data.description:
After my last article on the RENK Group (OTCPK:RNKGF), the company saw a non-trivial rate of outperformance despite my neutral rating on the business as an investment. The company is part of the current trends in defense, which are seeing defense contractors and related companies command higher and higher premiums due to significant interest in the rearmament of an entire continent, as well as the upside from the rest of the world for the companies that have international sectors and segments.

While RENK remains a solid business, my argument is that the market has, in part, started to recognize the significant and oft-unjustified premiums that these companies now trade at. Since its peak in July, the company's share price has declined, and even though it's still up by 28% in a relatively short time, the outperformance relative to SPY is now less than, or just at, 10% or so. You can find that last article here.

In this article, I will use the latest quarterly earnings to "zero in" on an updated price target and to further justify my expectations for RENK using the potential for an agreement of ceasefire agreement or the potential end of the war in Ukraine. It's my stance that such a development would severely impact not only RENK Group but every single European defense contractor or related party. That is why my general holdings in this sector are now very, very limited, taking profits not only in companies like Rheinmetall (OTCPK:RNMBF), but also in companies like Leonardo (OTCPK:FINMY) and the like.

I believe the appeal, for the moment, in terms of value is "over," and it is better to wait for a better entry point for more investment.

RENK Group: The Latest Results Do Not Support the Current Valuation or Exuberance

1H25 or the 2Q25 results have been out for about a week at this point. As of this time of writing, I seem to be the only analyst covering this company for Seeking Alpha - that part I do not quite understand, given the appeal of the business, but I do my best to highlight what I view as "undercovered" businesses with appeal to investors. And RENK certainly has appeal if you're buying it at the right price.

Growing order intakes and book-to-bill numbers are not the problem. The company continues to see double-digit growth in its order book, with an increase in book-to-bill of 0.3x in less than a year. The run rate of sales is now, at the latest numbers, close to €2B, which is almost double that of the 2023-2024 period.

The backlog, like with most defense companies, is at record high levels, for RENK at almost €6B, and the company will "live off" this for some time. Primary contributors were both the defense business, and the remaining business areas were also doing well.

As with many German businesses that do this, including Rheinmetall, RENK had for the longest time gotten used to getting most of its business from the civil market. That is a picture that has not only changed but turned around. The current split is a 74/26 to defense/civil, and the defense sector is by and large responsible for setting the tone.

The company also remains focused mostly on land-tech. The vehicle solutions are still 3-4x larger than marine, with new transmission sales level records. The Marine segment is more of an area where we are able to see a bit more in terms of headwinds.

And while the company has a bearings segment, that segment isn't really seeing growth comparable to any of the other segments or parts of RENK. Bearings grew less than 2.7% YoY. It's relatively clear that the less "military" something is, the more affected by the current macro the company becomes, and the more similar to your typical industrial this company becomes, seeing the same headwinds and patterns that companies like, say, KION (OTCPK:KIGRY) do. This is the first reason why I say that the current valuation for the business does not reflect what I view as the underlying potential.

Because, unless you believe that the current state of politics will go on forever, specifically in Europe (the company's main market), the question becomes not if there will be a change, but when and what effect that change would have on RENK.

The company has, as you can see in the backlog above, prepared itself well for that eventuality. Its backlog is well-filled, it has reinvested in the business for growth and expansion, and even in the case of a normalization of trade and politics in broader Europe, I do not think RENK Group will be the same as it was before the Russian invasion of Ukraine - just as most defense contractors have reinvested and grown. These improvements are clear through a variety of means, but chief among them are things like margin improvements and efficiencies.

The company's appeal is not in question here. That appeal has actually been obvious to me even before the invasion - that was the time to buy any defense contractor, though even I at the time thought the upside to be unclear. And I don't want to convey the picture that this company is "only EU"—because it's not. It does a lot of systems for the US navy and military as well, which are markets that will remain, likely at current levels, even going forward.

But my "thing" here becomes, what appeal does the company have when the defense industry, which is currently inarguably (based on historical multiples) expensive, normalizes?

The bullish camp would argue here that normalization, in the sense that the companies decline back down, will not come. At least not in the sense that I expect. This is because of things like AI, an increasingly tense geopolitical environment, and the like that are likely to see them trading at continued, high multiples due to steady growth. Some even argue that they offer good returns, going on to recommend them for income-oriented investors for the long term, and major banks and institutions continue to be positive here.

I say that this is a pipe dream, and one I have seen too often when it comes to any one sector that is "flying high," much like this one is currently doing.

Let's use the RENK Group as an example here to show you just how high these multiples have gone relative to any one appealing value stock that I currently elect to actually invest in.

First off, some normalization has happened. We're down from over 55x P/E to less than 46x P/E, which is a good start. However, it's 46x P/E for a company with a bare BB credit rating, a yield of less than 0.75%, and an average 5-year P/E multiple of 17x. The thesis is somewhat complicated by the fact that RENK Group, in its current iteration, is actually rather "fresh" on the stock market - it's only been on the market for about 1.5 years at this time. So rather than using historicals, we'll use peer multiples and average market returns to see where things might go.

However, it's important to realize that RENK has a long history of actually being on the stock market. It was listed in Frankfurt as early as 1923 but later became delisted. So this 2024 listing is more of a "return" to the stock market.

But let's look at the valuation.

RENK Group Upside Potential: It's Conservative as Can Be Here

I will use the bullish arguments to show you that even in the case of continued, 30%+ EPS growth on an annual basis with €1.04/share of 2024A, going to 2027E of €2.4/share, this company is overvalued and will not produce market-beating returns even at 30x P/E or slightly above. The current average weighted P/E is 45.26x. The forecasts currently given call for that improvement of 30% or above per year on continued growth. Even taking into account that we do not know how an end to the invasion of Ukraine would affect European end markets - or the valuation of these stocks—I want to point out that RENK has, during 2025, traded as low as 17-18x P/E. That, even with a 30% annualized growth rate, would in 2027E amount to a decline of 11% per year or below.

Even if we allow a BB-rated business to trade at over 30x P/E, the upside is not even 10% per year, inclusive of yield, not even at 30%+ annualized EPS growth rates. In fact, in order for the bullish argument to make any sense here, you'd have to argue that RENK should trade at somewhere close to 35-40x P/E.

Such valuations have stopped making sense for other defense contractors, and I don't believe they make sense for RENK either. Leonardo, as a prime example, is down to around 26x normalized after peaking over 30x. Rolls-Royce (OTCPK:RYCEF) is kind of still up there, but I've sold my stake quite some time ago, and I don't see how the company is going to maintain that valuation in the face of a declining growth rate (less than 15% estimated in 2026E). I believe American defense contractors offer a good future view into what can be expected for the European ones, and investors would do well in looking across the pond for an investment lesson in companies like Lockheed (LMT) and Northrop (NOC), both of which are trading below 25x P/E with far lower growth rates. Moreover, their US counterparts have better yields and better credit ratings.

My stance is that there's no long-term justification for this. Not AI, not continued geopolitical tension (unless we're talking all-out weaponized conflict in Europe, which I believe is a very remote possibility). I believe that there is only one possible, eventual outcome to what these companies are going to result in here on a forward basis - a significant valuation decline. When it is impossible to say - but I don't believe future growth will match the historical growth here.

Instead, I would value all defense companies at a higher-than-historical P/E, but not the current level we're seeing. In my previous article, I gave a €40/share PT and a fair value of about €55/share. This represents a 2026-2026E P/E of about 23-25x, by the way, with the growth estimates of 25-35% per year, which I consider not unlikely - I just consider the valuation to be unlikely to keep up at this pace over the long term. At below €40/share, you'd be ensured, at that fair value, an at least 15% annualized upside or above. At this valuation, you're either having to value a BB-rated company at above 35x P/E, or you're looking at something like a decline/sub-par return (or a significantly higher growth rate).

None of that is currently acceptable to me, and because of that, I view the company as a continued "HOLD" after the 1H25 period, based on the scenarios laid out above.

Risks to this thesis are as follows.

Risks to the Thesis

The obvious risk is continued outperformance. It's nigh-on impossible to speculate when the companies in this sector will "go down," as I believe they eventually will. If the journey continues, however, investors could still see massive returns despite high valuations. But the higher valuations go, the more extreme a crash can be. As hard as it is to invest in something that's being hated, it's in my experience even harder for us to "leave" behind a high-flying investment before a crash. It's the fear of missing out, or FOMO, as some call it.

That is the primary risk that I see here.

I will not change my PT but remain at €40/share, which for the ADR comes to $46.38/share, making the company a "HOLD".

Thesis

  • RENK Group AG is a very solid defense manufacturer, with over 70% of the company's annual revenue going directly to defense end markets. This has driven company earnings, order book, and forecasts up, as I believe too high. Also, the valuation is now above 30x P/E, which is very high for a BB-rated company with less than 1 year on the market in this iteration. (in this listing).
  • I view the business as fundamentally attractive, and I might regret my conservative take on the business from here on, but I cannot give the company any higher PT than €40/share due to the premiumization and what I view as overvaluation.
  • This PT means that you should buy the company at or below €40/share in order to get a good chance of a 15% annualized RoR, which means that the current rating is "Hold".

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative and well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansions/reversions.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which typically isn't enough. Based on this and a valuation target of €40/share, where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Hold" rating as of August 2025.
